title: Verify offline bundle signature during preflight (fail closed on invalid/untrusted)
preview: Context: As part of the Redwood Private on-prem/offline bundle signing initiative, the installer must verify bundle provenance before any install/upgrade steps run. This PR adds signature verification to the installer preflight and fails closed when the signature is invalid or the signing key is not trusted. This is the first enforcement point; trust-store/rotation and break-glass flows will land in follow-up PRs. What this does: - Adds a new preflight gate `verify-offline-bundle-signature` that runs after bundle discovery but before unpack/apply. - Verifies that `bundle/manifest.json` is present and canonicalizable, and that it contains a signature envelope (`signing` block) produced by the release pipeline. - Verifies the signature over the canonical manifest bytes using Ed25519 (Go stdlib `crypto/ed25519`). - Validates trust via a single pinned Redwood release public key shipped with the installer (temporary until trust store work in PR-24859). - Blocks install/upgrade on: missing signature, invalid signature, unsupported algorithm, or unknown key id. - Adds structured error codes so Support can triage consistently (see `docs/error-codes.md`). Canonicalization details (v0): - We canonicalize the manifest for verification by re-encoding JSON with stable key ordering and no insignificant whitespace. The implementation is intentionally strict and will error if unknown top-level keys are present in the `signing` block (to avoid signature bypass via ambiguous parsing). - The preflight prints the calculated manifest digest (sha256 of canonical bytes) for debugging. UX / CLI: - `redwood-private-installer preflight --bundle /path/to/bundle.tgz` now emits a clear PASS/FAIL result for signature verification. - On failure, we print: error code, reason, the expected key id (if present), and the log path. - No override flag is introduced in this PR (break-glass is a separate security decision; see ENG-4824). Testing: - Unit tests for: tampered manifest, mismatched signature, missing signing block, unknown key id. - A basic integration harness that runs preflight against a fixture bundle produced by `tools/make-test-bundle` (unsigned and signed variants). Notes / follow-ups: - Trust store + key rollover support: PR-24859. - Detached signature file layout: PR-24933. - Downgrade prevention using signed version metadata: PR-24902. - Improve error remediation messaging: PR-24873. Rollout consideration: This adds enforcement only when a bundle includes a `signing` block. For existing unsigned bundles used by customers, preflight will fail with a clear error; release engineering confirmed the next Private RC will ship signed bundles in parallel with an updated installer. Related: Linear ENG-4823 (installer preflight verification), ENG-4821 (manifest canonicalization), ENG-4822 (pipeline signing step).

title: Selective component vendoring and federated-registry seed tool for air-gapped installs
preview:
Motivation: customers running Redwood Private in highly constrained air-gapped sites asked for smaller, auditable offline bundles and a reproducible way to seed multiple local registries (federated seed) when performing staged rollouts across disconnected clusters. This PR introduces a selective component vendoring strategy and a small, focused registry-seed CLI that can bootstrap multiple registries from a shrinkwrapped manifest.

What this changes:
- Selective component vendoring: a new manifest layer (component-shrinkwrap.yaml) allows operators to opt-in to vendor only the runtime components they need (kubelet, containerd, cni, crictl, kernel-modules) instead of the entire distro image set. The installer will generate slim OCI bundles accordingly, reducing offline bundle size by up to ~60% in sample overlays.
- Federated registry seed tool: a new binary (installer/cmd/seed-registry) that reads a vendored index and performs concurrent, resumable pushes to multiple local registries with content verification and checksum reconciliation. It supports HTTP registry proxies and atomic promotion tags for staged rollouts.
- Terraform module tweaks: updates to terraform/modules/registry to expose per-component credentials and a optional bootstrap lifecycle that integrates with the seed tool.
- Helm hooks and charts: installer/charts/airgap tweaks to add a lightweight preflight Job that validates the presence + signatures of vendored components and a post-upgrade hook that can trigger an idempotent registry reconciliation.

Migration notes: existing customers using full-bundle installation are unaffected by default. To adopt selective vendoring, run the new `redwood-installer generate-shrinkwrap --components kubelet,containerd,cni` to create a component-shrinkwrap.yaml and then build an overlay. The seed tool requires a credentials file in the registry module (see docs).

Testing and validation:
- Unit: new unit tests cover shrinkwrap parsing, component selection heuristics, and OCI index generation.
- Integration: ran seed tool against a 3-registry federated testbed (minio-backed registries) and validated image checksums and promotion behavior under simulated network interruptions.
- E2E: smoke install against K3s and RKE2 variants with selective vendoring enabled.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: operator misconfiguration may produce bundles missing required kernel modules for certain distros. Mitigation: installer will run a compatibility check and include a per-distro required-component whitelist; the preflight Job blocks install if critical components are missing.
- Risk: concurrent pushes to registries can be throttled by very small airgap appliances. Mitigation: seed tool exposes concurrency and retry knobs and defaults to conservative values when target registries report latency.

Rollout plan: merge to main; stage as a minor opt-in feature in the next nightly release; collect telemetry from opt-in installs and iterate.

Checklist: 
- [x] implementation
- [x] unit & integration tests
- [x] docs (installer/docs/component-vendoring.md and registry-seed.md)
- [x] changelog entry

Notes: This PR focuses on operational ergonomics for constrained sites rather than multi-arch duplication. Multi-arch vendoring will be left to a follow-up where we integrate delta indexing and cross-arch promotion.

title: operator-image-pinning-and-postrollback-validation-hooks
preview:
Motivation: In several recent customer upgrade exercises we observed instability caused by operator images floating between upgrades and by silent chart metadata drift that allowed incompatible post-rollback states. This PR introduces three complementary safeguards: (1) operator image pinning via digest lockfiles surfaced in both the installer manifests and Terraform module outputs; (2) a chart sub-release stamping mechanism so the installer can detect and refuse upgrades that change sub-release semantics without an explicit migration plan; (3) a Helm post-rollback validation hook that runs lightweight data-plane health checks and fails the rollback (leaving operator in quarantined mode) if core invariants are violated.

What I changed (summary): Added an image lockfile generator and validation flow to the installer CLI; extended terraform/modules/installer to accept and export image_digest values and refuse plan/apply when the lockfile is stale; modified Helm charts to include a chartSubRelease annotation and collector logic in the operator bootstrap; added a post-rollback Helm hook that executes a small job which runs sanity checks (DB schema version, KV cache presence, background worker connectivity). Added docs: upgrade-runbook and operator-image-lockfile guide. Added e2e upgrade job to CI that runs a staged upgrade -> rollback -> validation flow to exercise the new hooks. Implemented a small migration guard in the installer that annotates resources with schemaVersion markers and will block a downgrade if the on-disk schema version is greater than the target release.

Commit highlights (high level): - add: installer/cmd/lockfile: generator + validator - add: terraform/modules/installer/image-lock.tf + outputs - add: helm/templates/hooks/post-rollback-validate.yaml - change: helm/Chart.yaml (new chartSubRelease field and annotation processing) - change: installer/upgrade/guard: schema-version preflight checks - add: docs/upgrade-runbook.md and docs/operator-image-lockfile.md - add: ci/e2e/upgrade-rollback-flow.yaml - tests: integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go and unit tests for lockfile validation

Files changed (high level): helm/Chart.yaml, helm/values.schema.json, helm/templates/hooks/post-rollback-validate.yaml, installer/cmd/lockfile/*.go, installer/upgrade/guard.go, terraform/modules/installer/image-lock.tf, docs/upgrade-runbook.md, docs/operator-image-lockfile.md, ci/e2e/upgrade-rollback-flow.yaml, integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go, tests/fixtures/*, scripts/bin/lockfile-generator.sh

Implementation notes: - Image pinning: the installer generates a lockfile mapping component -> image@sha256:digest. The terraform module consumes a lockfile path and exposes image_digest outputs so upgrades pin the operator/controller image. During a dry-run the installer will fail if current cluster images differ from lockfile (opt-in override supported for emergency operations). - Chart sub-release stamping: Chart.yaml gains a new optional field chartSubRelease. The operator bootstraps will compare stored sub-release (in ConfigMap metadata) with the incoming chartSubRelease and require an explicit migration when the stamp increases in a way that implies structural change. - Post-rollback validation: Implemented as a Helm post-rollback hook that triggers a short-lived pod which runs a small binary (included in operator image) to validate DB schema, consumer queue health, and cached KV consistency. If validation fails the hook marks the release with label rollbackValidation=failed and operator enters quarantine mode and emits a high-severity event. - Downgrade guard: Installer upgrade path now sets a schemaVersion marker on the cluster; the preflight will fail if targetVersion < schemaVersion and the user has not supplied --allow-downgrade with an explicit migration plan.

Testing & CI: Unit tests for lockfile parsing/validation (installer tests), integration harness that runs a create-upgrade-rollback scenario (integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go), and a new e2e pipeline job runs a small cluster in KinD that exercises the lockfile + post-rollback hook path. CI summary: initial run failed due to flaky timing in the hook job; fixed by adding a 30s readiness probe and retry loop. Final CI: all checks pass.

Migration plan & rollout: This PR is safe to merge to main but requires a coordinated rollout: - Step 1 (canary): merge and enable image lockfile generation for internal staging only; do not enforce failure on stale lockfile (log-only). - Step 2: enable enforcement for beta clusters with a migration-runbook run. - Step 3: enable enforcement by default and surface upgrade failures via Console. For private deployments customers can opt-in to enforcement by supplying the lockfile to the terraform module.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior is non-breaking (lockfile generation and chartSubRelease are optional). Enabling enforcement is opt-in until Step 3 described above. Downgrade guard is conservative and requires explicit --allow-downgrade to proceed.

Risks & mitigations: - Risk: operator image digests drift causing unexpected failures for customers who rely on image tags. Mitigation: we provide an automated lockfile generator and the installer supports a staged rollout with an opt-in enforcement flag. - Risk: post-rollback hook could cause longer rollback times. Mitigation: validation is intentionally lightweight (<60s typical) and can be disabled per-release via annotation for emergency rollbacks.

Related work: previous PRs added manifest hashes and version lockfiles; this complements them by publishing digest pins, adding chart sub-release semantics, and automating validation after rollback. See related Linear items: ENG-5124 (upgrade safety), ENG-5178 (helm rollback validation).

Notes for reviewers: focus on the lockfile format, terraform consumption surface, and the failure semantics of the post-rollback hook. The hook uses the operator image binary (validate-bridge) which already exists in the image; integration tests exercise failure and success paths.

Merge outcome: squash-merged into main after approvals and CI green.

title: Add KMS preflight validation, permission checks, and audit-friendly validation report
preview: Context: We keep seeing Private installs fail late (or worse: succeed but later decrypt fails) due to KMS misconfiguration: wrong key ARN/region, missing IAM actions, key disabled, policy doesn’t allow the installer principal, or customer KMS throttling. This PR adds an explicit KMS preflight step to the Private installer so we fail fast with actionable output, and we produce an audit-friendly validation report that customers can attach to change tickets/security evidence. Summary of changes (high level): 1) New installer preflight stage: "kms" runs by default when KMS envelope encryption is configured (and can also be invoked explicitly via `redwood-private-installer validate kms`). 2) Permission + configuration checks with actionable remediation: resolves KMS key identifier, validates region alignment, validates key state (enabled, not pending deletion), and performs a safe Encrypt/Decrypt round-trip using a short-lived random payload (never persisted). The checks are mapped into a small set of installer error codes and messages that line up with the platform-wide KMS error taxonomy (AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, DisabledKey). 3) Audit/ops report generation: on every run (success or failure), write a structured JSON report to `./reports/kms-preflight-<timestamp>.json` (override via `--report-path`). Report includes: installer version/build, timestamp, deployment id/cluster name (if available), cloud provider, key identifier (redacted by default), principal ARN/subject (redacted by default), each check result, latency per call, AWS request IDs (or equivalent), and a top-level pass/fail summary. Sensitive fields are excluded (no plaintext, no ciphertext, no auth headers, no full policy documents). 4) Guardrails: `--skip-kms-preflight` remains available for break-glass/airgapped bootstrap flows, but now requires `--i-understand-kms-risk` and emits a prominent warning in stdout and the report. 5) Support ergonomics: error output now includes “what we tried”, “what failed”, and “how to fix” sections with copy/paste IAM action lists, plus a hint to attach the report file when opening a support ticket. Implementation notes: - AWS KMS: uses DescribeKey + Encrypt/Decrypt round-trip. We intentionally do NOT call GenerateDataKey to avoid requiring broader permissions than runtime decrypt/encrypt needs in some deployments; the installer validates the minimum runtime envelope path. - Timeouts/retries: preflight uses conservative timeouts (2s per call, max 2 retries w/ jitter) to avoid long hangs during install. Throttling is reported clearly with suggested next steps (quota/limit, backoff, or allowlist of installer IP if applicable). - Redaction: by default key ARN is truncated to last 12 chars, principal is truncated to account + role name; `--report-include-identifiers` will include full identifiers if the operator chooses (still excludes secrets). - Output format stability: report JSON includes a `schema_version` field for future parsing by audit pipelines. Testing: - Unit tests for redaction, error mapping, and report serialization. - A small mocked KMS client harness that simulates AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/DisabledKey and asserts installer output and report contents. - CI adds a smoke run for `validate kms` on a stubbed config (no real cloud creds) to ensure the stage wiring doesn’t regress. Operator-facing behavior (examples): - Missing permission -> installer fails with: "KMS preflight failed: AccessDenied on Decrypt. Ensure the installer principal has kms:Decrypt on <key>. See report: reports/kms-preflight-...json" and includes a minimal IAM snippet. - Wrong region -> installer fails with: "KMS key region mismatch: key is in us-west-2 but deployment configured for us-east-1". - Throttling -> installer fails with: "KMS throttling detected (429/ThrottlingException). Reduce parallelism, check KMS quotas, or retry later. See report for request IDs and latencies." Checklist: [x] Backwards compatible (new preflight only runs when KMS is enabled; skip flag available) [x] Report contains no secrets and is safe to attach to change tickets [x] Error messages include remediation hints [x] Added tests for key failure modes. Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Hook report export into the broader "evidence pack" generation flow (see ENG-4826). - Add GCP/Azure native KMS provider implementations in installer (today we validate AWS KMS path; other providers remain config-validated only).

title: Introduce KMS Federation Operator + SealedSecret Guardian for cross-cluster HSM key propagation
preview: Context: customers deploying Redwood Private across multiple air-gapped datacenters requested automated, auditable propagation of HSM-wrapped keys and TLS certs between primary KMS instances and read-only local KMS proxies. This PR introduces a KMS Federation Operator (KFO) plus a SealedSecret Guardian daemonset to orchestrate safe key material replication, reconciled by Terraform/Helm hooks. Motivation: avoid manual sealed secret unseal workflows during cluster join, reduce time-to-recovery for HSM failovers, and provide an auditable policy-based propagation model. Scope: operator CRDs + controller, Helm chart changes (upgrade hooks, post-install job), Terraform module for federation resources, upgrade/rollback safe hooks, docs and test harness. title: vendor rke2 CNI plugins + offline upgrade/rollback hooks for air-gapped installs
preview: Motivation: Customers running Redwood Private on-prem with RKE2 often operate fully air-gapped clusters and require a bundled, validated CNI set plus deterministic upgrade/rollback behavior during offline upgrades. This PR vendors a minimal set of RKE2-compatible CNI artifacts (canal/flannel + calico dataplane bits and rke2-cni shim) into the offline bundle, adds a small SELinux module and policy packaging step required for RHEL environments, and introduces an idempotent offline upgrade/rollback hook framework so operators can perform safe rollbacks without internet access. What changed (high-level): 1) Packaging: added vendor/cni/ directory with compressed tarballs for canal and calico dataplane, rke2-cni shim, and SHA256 checksums. 2) Installer runtime: extend airgap preflight to verify CNI artifact signatures and space checks, and to unpack only the CNI payload needed by the target kube-distro. 3) Hooks: new hooks/upgrade-preflight.sh, hooks/upgrade-postinstall.sh, hooks/rollback.sh that run entirely offline; hooks implement graceful drain/uncordon, KV cache preservation for Redwood services, and automated iptables/ebtables cleanup. 4) SELinux: small rpm-style policy bundle and a conditional module loader used only on RHEL/OL family hosts. 5) Helm and manifests: a patched charts/redwood-system chart to allow using local image/asset references for CNI DaemonSet and to expose a cni.install.strategy value (bundle|host). 6) Tests & docs: integration test harness updates and an ops-guide section documenting offline upgrade/rollback commands and failure modes. Commits summary: - add vendor/cni payloads and checksums - implement offline preflight and unpack logic - add selinux module packaging and loader - add offline hook framework and idempotent rollback script - patch helm chart to accept local cni references - add integration tests and docs Notes and rationale: - We picked canal (flannel+calico) and a trimmed calico dataplane set because they cover the networking/mtu/ipam modes used by most customers and keep the vendor bundle size reasonable. - The rollback hook is intentionally conservative: it first attempts a fast rollback using Helm release history where available, then falls back to manual manifest restore using a timestamped snapshot kept in /var/lib/redwood/installer/snapshots. - SELinux module is optional at runtime; installer will skip and emit a warning on distros that don't support semodule. Files changed (high-level): - vendor/cni/canal-1.14.0.tar.gz.sha256 - vendor/cni/canal-1.14.0.tar.gz - vendor/cni/calico-dataplane-3.25.0.tar.gz - installer/preflight/checks_cni.go (new checks) - installer/runtime/unpack_airgap.go (unpack selector changes) - hooks/upgrade-preflight.sh - hooks/upgrade-postinstall.sh - hooks/rollback.sh - packaging/selinux/redwood_cni.pp - charts/redwood-system/values.yaml (cni.install.strategy) - charts/redwood-system/templates/cni-daemonset.yaml (local image refs) - integration/tests/airgap_cni_upgrade_test.sh - docs/ops/offline-upgrades.md Testing performed: - Local RKE2 clusters (v1.25 and v1.26) in air-gapped VM lab: full upgrade flow (install → upgrade → rollback) exercised end-to-end on Ubuntu 22.04 and RHEL 9. - SELinux module load/unload tested on RHEL 9.1. - CI: unit tests + integration script run in a simulated airgap (--local-bundle flag). Observed CI was flaky initially due to checksum handling; fixed by switching to streaming SHA256 verification and re-running pipelines. Security considerations: - All vendored artifacts are accompanied by SHA256 checksums and an internal GPG signature (keys stored in the private installer signing keyset). The installer enforces signature verification by default for air-gapped installs; operators can pass --insecure for lab usage. - The SELinux policy is minimal and scoped to the installer and CNI sockets; no broad permissions granted. Upgrade/rollback operator steps (short): 1) Place offline bundle on installer host and run ./install.sh --bundle /path/to/bundle --kube-distro rke2 2) To upgrade: ./installerctl upgrade --bundle /t/bundle --hooks enable 3) To rollback: ./installerctl rollback --to-snapshot 2025-11-18--21:04 4) If automatic rollback fails, manual restore path documented in docs/ops/offline-upgrades.md Related Linear/Jira: ENG-7421 (airgap CNI vendoring), ENG-7530 (rollback hooks), PM-1182 (ops guide). Release notes: - Air-gapped installs: vendor canal+calico dataplane for RKE2; verify artifacts offline - Installer will now expose cni.install.strategy and honor local CNI references for air-gapped clusters - Add offline upgrade/rollback hooks for safer on-prem upgrades - SELinux module packaging for RHEL-based hosts Known limitations: - Bundle size increases by ~120MB. We plan to move to delta artifact delivery in a follow-up (ENG-7689). - Currently validated for RKE2 and kubeadm; K3s support requires a follow-up patch to handle k3s-specific CNI shims. Merge and rollout: Squash-merged to main after approvals from Arman and Priya and passing CI. Backported docs and a brief ops note were included in the release checklist.

title: add-central-log-shipper-and-dashboard-migration-tooling-for-private-installs
preview: Adds a centralized log shipping option (promtail -> S3 -> loki indexer) as an optional subchart to the private-installer Helm bundle, a terraform module to provision long-term object storage and Grafana workspace configuration, and a one-shot dashboard migration utility that performs a schema-safe migration of existing Grafana dashboards during upgrades. Motivation: customers deploying Private need a reliable, upgrade-safe path to connect cluster logs and persisted dashboards into the observability stack without manual intervention. This PR implements the Helm templates and values schema, the Terraform module that creates an S3-compatible bucket with lifecycle rules and IAM policy, a k8s Job-based migration runner that performs dashboard diffs and backouts, unit and e2e tests for the chart and migration tool, and documentation + an upgrade checklist. The description below summarizes commits, changed areas, CI outcomes, and review thread.

title: ci: generate release graph and manifest validator for private-installer artifacts
preview: Motivation: CI for private installer releases lacked a machine-readable release graph and a manifest-level validator that ties signed artifacts (images, helm charts, tf modules) back to the release manifest. This PR adds a release-graph generator and a manifest-validator stage to the private-installer CI pipeline to ensure provenance, enable safer upgrades/rollbacks, and block unsigned/unaligned manifests from publishing. Summary of changes: - new pipeline step 'build:release-graph' which produces a directed release graph (JSON) that maps artifacts -> helm chart values -> terraform module versions and includes cryptographic digests and SBOM references; graph is collated from build outputs and attestation metadata - new pipeline step 'validate:manifest' that consumes a release graph and enforces: cosign signature verification (keyless and KMS), SBOM presence, chart values checksum, and compatibility matrix checks for upgrades - small helper library under tools/release-graph that normalizes artifact metadata, computes canonical digests, and emits signatures-ready metadata for the signer job - Helm chart linting rules extended to emit a Helm manifest fingerprint (stable ordering) and surface mismatches between chart contents and release graph - Terraform module manifest extraction to include provider pins and a short policy check to prevent unsupported provider upgrades in the same release - CI orchestration changes: the validate step runs before artifact promotion and blocks publishing to the release bucket on failure - added an automated delta-rollback manifest generator which creates a minimal rollback plan from the release graph when a canary fails Why this is different: existing pipelines stamp artifacts and upload indexes, but we need a top-level release graph that can be consumed by Private control-plane for deterministic rollouts and by on-prem installers to reconcile manifests during upgrades. This PR focuses on making the release artifact a first-class, verifiable graph rather than loose indexes. Checklist: - [x] release-graph generator implemented - [x] manifest validator implemented and integrated into the CI workflow - [x] cosign verification (keyless and KMS) wired into validator - [x] Helm fingerprinting integrated - [x] Terraform module extraction implemented - [x] unit tests for tools/release-graph - [x] e2e run in staging that simulates signed artifact promotion - [x] added docs for ops on how to interpret the release graph Changes included (high level): tools/release-graph/* (new library and CLI), ci/pipeline/build-release-graph.yaml (new job), ci/pipeline/validate-manifest.yaml (new job), charts/private-installer/patches/manifest-fingerprint.yaml (helper), terraform/modules/release-metadata/README.md (docs), scripts/delta-rollback/gen-rollback.sh (new helper), tests/integration/release-graph-e2e (e2e harness). Testing and verification: - unit coverage for release-graph normalization functions - integration test that generates a release graph from staged artifacts, signs them with a test KMS key, then runs validator (CI job uses real cosign verification in staging) - staging rollout run that exercised the delta-rollback generator after a simulated canary failure - CI history shows two failed validator runs during early iteration (missing SBOMs) which were fixed by the chart/lint rule update Rollout plan: - merged to main and enabled in staging for one week; manual gating for production release (ops approval required) - enable automatic blocking on validator failures for subsequent releases; until then validator failures are opt-in via release-note flag Migration notes: - existing release index consumers must be updated to prefer release-graph.json; a small compatibility shim is provided under tools/release-graph/bin/compatify - operators must ensure cosign public key or KMS access is available in the Private control-plane to verify signatures Security considerations: - cosign key usage requires KMS scopes; the validator supports both keyless (OIDC) verification and KMS-backed verification; by default validator rejects unsigned artifacts - SBOM presence is mandatory for private installer releases; policy can be relaxed via a per-customer policy file if needed Documentation: - docs/private-installer/release-graph.md added explaining format and how to use the delta-rollback generator - ops playbook updated with steps to manually validate a release graph Known limitations & follow-ups: - release-graph currently serializes all digests in SHA256; we will add multi-algo support (ENG-6780 follow-up) - we do not yet have an automated remediation when validator fails (ENG-6723 will track a retry/auto-fix job) Related: ENG-6723 (release graph spec), ENG-6780 (multi-algo hashing), SUP-3048 (support ticket where customer asked for deterministic rollback plans).

title: external-kms-plugin-and-cert-sync-controller-for-onprem-rotation
preview: Motivation: private installations need a way to integrate external KMS (cloud or on-prem HSM gateways) without forcing Vault-as-first-class and to keep TLS artifacts synchronized across namespaces during key rotation. This PR introduces two coordinated changes: 1) an optional installer hook that deploys a lightweight external-kms-plugin sidecar and Terraform snippets to register external KMS endpoints (KMS plugin config + policy templates); 2) a cert-sync-controller (Kubernetes controller) that watches KMS-backed key metadata and existing TLS secrets (including sealed-secrets-wrapped secrets) to perform automated, atomic certificate replacements across the cluster during rotation. Goals: reduce manual rotation steps for Private deployments, support customer-managed KMS/HSM, and provide a safe upgrade path with canaries and rollbacks. The description below outlines implementation details, migration plan, and risk mitigations. Implementation summary: - Installer/terraform: new optional module `external_kms` in terraform/ modules that wires up provider endpoints, validates TLS with provided CA bundle, and outputs Kubernetes Secret manifests for connector config. - Helm: chart changes add `externalKms.enabled` flag, sidecar container image references, RBAC rules for the cert-sync-controller, and a new `preupgrade` hook to validate existing TLS assets before applying new certs. - Controller: `cert-sync-controller` (Go) runs as a Deployment; it subscribes to a KMSConfig CRD and Secret events, uses signed key fingerprints to detect rotation candidates, generates CSR via local signer when allowed, and rotates target Secrets (supports sealed-secrets encrypted payloads by unlocking via the installer-provided sealing key). - Backwards compatibility: all changes are gated behind `externalKms.enabled=false` by default; controller is opt-in. - Rollout: default canary to 5% of nodes via nodeSelector + label-based canary, automated healthchecks (liveness/readiness + smoke requests) and automatic rollback on certificate validation failures. - Testing: unit tests for controller logic, integration e2e in the benchmark-lab that exercises a simulated KMS gateway, and manual on-prem validation (steps included in docs). See release notes + upgrade instructions below. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module with input/output docs - [x] Helm chart templates and hook - [x] CRD + controller implementation - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Operator RBAC and PSP/PodSecurity verification - [x] Docs and upgrade guide - [x] Canary rollout strategy and rollback hooks Related: builds on work in ENG-4000 (vault auto-unseal) and complements sealed-secrets automation (does not replace Vault). Security: we require KMS endpoints to authenticate via mTLS or a signed token; the installer will validate CA bundles and refuses to enable the controller if authentication tests fail. For sealed-secrets, controller will only decrypt using cluster sealing key provided explicitly by the admin during install (no implicit key retrieval). Migration notes: - Upgrading from 2.3 -> 2.4: operator will detect existing TLS secrets and run a dry-run validation; admin must approve the first rotation via the console or CLI. - Rollback: chart includes helm hooks which will restore previous Secret manifests (helm rollback) and controller will stop rotating if `externalKms.enabled=false`. Observability: new metrics (redwood_cert_sync_rotations_total, redwood_cert_sync_errors, redwood_kms_plugin_sync_latency) and tracing spans (controller reconcile) added to console dashboards. Rollout plan: stage to Dedicated beta clusters for 2 weeks, then enable canary for 5% of customer on-prem clusters with customer opt-in, gather telemetry, then full release. See release_notes for more details.

title: tenant-probe-injector-and-slo-synthesizer-for-private-installs
preview:
Motivation and summary: This PR adds a tenant-aware probe injector and an SLO synthesizer to the Private Installer. The goal is to provide out-of-the-box synthetic checks and tenant-scoped SLOs/dashboards for Prometheus + Grafana in private/VPC deployments without forcing per-tenant manual configuration. This is targeted at customers who want predictable SLOs and early detection of network or configuration regressions across tenant boundaries.

What it contains: 1) A Helm subchart 'probe-injector' that deploys a small sidecar injector controller and a configurable Prometheus Blackbox Exporter + probe CRDs. The sidecar injector can attach a lightweight HTTP/TCP/ICMP probe sidecar to tenant-facing pods in the private cluster via annotations applied by the installer. 2) An 'slo-synthesizer' component (Go binary + controller) that consumes tenant intent (SLO targets stored in a namespaced ConfigMap generated by the installer or Terraform) and emits: - Prometheus recording rules and alerting rules for error and latency SLOs - Grafana dashboard JSON models per tenant with templated panels and scoreboard SLO summaries - An evaluation dashboard that aggregates tenant SLO burn rates. 3) Terraform module changes: optional probe registration resource and a new variable set to control per-tenant probe windows and scrape targets when provisioning Private instances via IaC. 4) Installer UX additions: new flags --enable-tenant-probes and --tenant-slo-defaults-file to toggle and supply defaults. 5) Upgrade/rollback safety: new canary annotations and a two-phase rollout in the Helm chart plus a migration job that can be re-run safely on rollback. 6) Tests and docs: unit tests for the synthesizer logic, integration smoke test in the installer CI to validate probe creation and rule generation, and a new docs page under docs/observability/tenant-probes.md.

Commits (high-level): - feat: add probe-injector helm subchart and CRD manifests - feat: implement slo-synthesizer controller + Go library for rule/dashboard generation - feat: add terraform module hooks for probe registration - chore: installer flags and config plumbing - ci: add integration smoke test to installer pipeline - docs: tenant probes and SLO synthesizer guide - fix: rollout hooks and rollback-safe migration job

Files changed (high level): - helm/probe-injector/* (Chart, templates, CRDs, notes) - installer/cmd/* (flags and config parsing) - installer/terraform/modules/probes/* (new module + examples) - controllers/slo-synthesizer/* (controller, rule generator, unit tests) - docs/observability/tenant-probes.md - ci/integration/installer-probe-smoke.yml - charts/redwood-private-installer/values.schema.json (new keys)

Design notes and constraints: - The injector is annotation-driven to remain opt-in; installer will annotate pods that map to tenant ingress by default when --enable-tenant-probes is set. - Probes use Blackbox Exporter to centralize probe types; exporters are deployed per-node-pool in larger installs to avoid cross-tenant noisy neighbors. - SLO synthesis uses conservative defaults (p95 latency objective, 99.9% success rate) that are documented and overrideable per tenant. - Rules and dashboards are namespaced to tenant so Grafana folder-per-tenant conventions can be kept. - We intentionally avoid storing tenant secrets in the synthesizer; it only reads ConfigMaps and emits Prometheus/Grafana objects.

Review conversation (summary): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Can we make the probe frequency configurable per-tenant and add a safety cap to avoid overloading small clusters?" Author reply: "Yes — added terraform and installer knobs with sane defaults and a global rate-limiter in the injector. See terraform/modules/probes/README and charts notes." - Lena Fischer: "Alerting: how do we prevent duplicate alerts across tenant and cluster-level rules?" Author reply: "Synthesizer marks tenant rules with tenant labels and a `slo_scope=tenant`; cluster-level rules include `slo_scope=cluster`. Alertmanager routing is configured in a follow-up to route tenant alerts to tenant channels. Added tests to ensure no identical rule names clash across namespaces." - Omar Khan (approval after changes): "Verified rollout hooks and smoke test locally; incremental upgrade path looks safe." - CI comments: initial CI run flagged linting issues in helm templates; author fixed and re-ran. Integration smoke test initially flaky due to timing; added a readiness probe and extended wait-time in CI test.

CI status: Full pipeline green on final run (unit, helm lint, terraform fmt/validate, installer integration smoke test).

Backward compatibility & upgrade notes: - Feature is opt-in; default behavior for existing private installs is unchanged unless the new flags are set. - Migration job runs once during upgrade if tenant SLO defaults are present; it is idempotent and safe to re-run on rollback. - Helm chart bump is non-breaking and follows semantic versioning; migration steps documented in release notes.

Security & operational considerations: - The probe injector runs with minimized privileges; it only mutates pod annotations and creates probe resources in tenant namespaces. - Prometheus rules generated are limited in cardinality; synthesizer enforces label cardinality checks. - Grafana dashboard provisioning uses folder-per-tenant tokens which are rotated via the installer-managed KMS integration.

Merge outcome: Merged via squash-and-merge after approvals and green CI. Merge commit contains summarized changelog and upgrade notes.

Follow-ups (planned): - ENG-8421: PagerDuty routing for tenant SLO alerts and runbook links (linked). - SRE-309: Add cost-based probe sampling to reduce token and network usage in very large installs. - Small RFC to expose SLO targets in the Console UI for hosted parity.

Notes for release: included in next private-installer minor release; documented in release_notes and operational playbook.

title: introduce-crossplane-driven-kms-provisioning-and-tls-orchestrator-for-onprem
preview:
Summary: Add a Crossplane-backed provisioning flow and a TLS orchestration component that coordinates HSM/KMS key lifecycle events with Helm/Terraform upgrades for private deployments.
Motivation: customers asked for an automated path to provision cloud-backed KMS instances (and on-prem HSM proxies) using Crossplane so the Private Installer can own end-to-end lifecycle for tenant keys during cluster bootstrapping and upgrades. This also reduces manual steps during TLS certificate rotations and prevents drift between sealed-secrets/Vault state and KMS key metadata.
Design: 1) crossplane/controller: new CRD 'KMSProvision' that maps to provider resources (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or an HSM-proxy Deployment); 2) provisioning module: terraform shim and new Crossplane composition templates shipped in helm chart's templates/crossplane/; 3) tls-orchestrator daemonset: a small controller that watches KMSProvision and Secret resources, coordinates staged key rotation (create new key, copy metadata, re-encrypt sealed secrets, rollout server certs), and emits progress metrics; 4) upgrade hooks: helm pre-upgrade/post-upgrade hooks talk to the orchestrator to ensure staged rollback-safe rotations; 5) installer flag: --enable-crossplane-kms and --crossplane-namespace to opt in.
Upgrade/rollback: orchestrator implements a three-phase rotation (provision -> mirror -> switch) with safe rollback markers written to a ClusterConfig CR. If a helm upgrade fails, the orchestrator halts switch phase and leaves old keys active; an automated rollback script reverts sealed-secret rewrites.
Security: all Crossplane compositions created with least-privilege roles; orchestrator talks to KMS with short-lived certs issued by cluster CA; HSM proxy path supports PKCS#11 or gRPC plugin.
Testing: unit tests for controller logic, e2e test harness that runs a mini Crossplane control plane in-kind, and upgrade-scenario integration tests against the private installer testbeds. Backwards compatibility: opt-in only, existing clusters unaffected unless flagged.
Rollout plan: feature gated in installer 1.18.2, developer preview in sandbox clusters, followed by canary on two customer VPCs.
Related work: ENG-5732 (crossplane provisioning), ENG-5789 (tls-orchestrator design).
Checklist: - [x] design doc linked - [x] crossplane composition templates added - [x] terraform shim and examples - [x] helm chart changes with hooks - [x] orchestrator daemonset + RBAC - [x] unit & e2e tests - [x] integration test for rollback scenarios - [x] documentation updates in docs/private/kms-crossplane.md
Commits: ["9c4a1b5 add KMSProvision CRD and controller scaffolding","fa2d3b7 helm: add crossplane composition templates and values","b8f7e6c tls-orchestrator: initial daemonset and controller reconciler","d3f4a2a terraform: add shim for crossplane provider binding","e1a2b77 tests: add e2e upgrade/rollback scenario","c4b2d90 docs: installer flags and migration guide"]
Changed files (high-level): ["charts/private-installer/templates/crossplane/compositions.yaml","charts/private-installer/templates/crossplane/rolebinding.yaml","charts/private-installer/templates/hooks/pre-upgrade-orchestrator.yaml","charts/private-installer/templates/orchestrator/daemonset.yaml","installer/cmd/bootstrapping/kms_provision.go","installer/flags.go","terraform/crossplane-shim/main.tf","pkg/orchestrator/reconciler.go","pkg/orchestrator/rotation_state.go","pkg/orchestrator/kms_client_pkcs11.go","tests/e2e/upgrade_rotation_test.go","docs/private/kms-crossplane.md"]
Testing & CI: GitHub Actions matrix running unit, lint, k8s-integration (KinD + Crossplane), and onprem smoke tests. Initial run showed a flaky e2e test with a timeout; adjusted wait loops and re-ran. All checks green at merge time.
Review conversation: "Marcus Li: please add the failure injection test for step 2 (mirror) - we need to assert partial rewrites don't leave secrets unusable.", "Evan Delgado: added failure-injection harness and test; updated reconciler to checkpoint intermediate state to ClusterConfig.", "Aisha Khan: RBAC looks wide in the first patch, can we scope to the crossplane namespace?", "Evan Delgado: narrowed RoleBindings to the provided crossplane namespace; added adapter to request namespaced role when provider is namespaced.", "Priya Natarajan: approved after verifying terraform shim does not expose credentials in plan output.", "Marcus Li: approved - leaving note to watch KMS request rates during canary."
Merge: merged via squash, merge commit message references ENG-5732 and ENG-5789.
Post-merge actions: created follow-up issue to add observability metrics for rotation durations and failure counts.
Notes for operators: to enable in an existing cluster set installer flag --enable-crossplane-kms and provide cloud provider credentials via the Crossplane provider secret in the configured crossplane namespace. If using on-prem HSM proxy, set values.hsmProxy.enabled=true and provide PKCS#11 config. End of PR.

title: Private observability: add preflight checks + post-install validation for Prometheus scrapes and Grafana dashboards
preview:
## Summary This PR adds installer-level observability guardrails for Redwood Private installs (VPC + on-prem), focused on the “Private observability minimum pack” rollout. It introduces (1) preflight checks to catch the most common misconfigurations that lead to blank dashboards / missing alerts (storage class, PVC sizing, NetworkPolicy restrictions, resource requests) and (2) a post-install validation step that verifies Prometheus scrapes and Grafana dashboard import succeeded. The goal is to make installs more deterministic for customer platform teams and reduce Support escalations like SUP-49210.

## Motivation / context We’ve seen repeated failures where the installer completes successfully but the observability pack is effectively broken: Prometheus can’t scrape due to restrictive NetworkPolicies, Prometheus/Grafana PVCs can’t bind in customer clusters, or Grafana dashboards never import because the sidecar/loader can’t reach the API. In hosted environments we have stronger environmental guarantees; in Private, the installer should surface actionable guidance before and immediately after install.

## What’s included Preflight checks (new `installer preflight observability` stage, also runs as part of `install` when enabled): - Storage: confirm a writable StorageClass exists (or user supplied `observability.storageClassName`), validate PVC access mode compatibility, validate requested sizes are >= minimums. - Resources: sanity check requests/limits for Prometheus + Grafana + log collector against node allocatable (best-effort; warns if cluster appears undersized). - Networking: detect whether NetworkPolicies are present in the target namespace and (if enabled) validate egress/ingress needed for Prometheus to scrape Redwood services (TCP/metrics ports) and for Grafana to reach Prometheus datasource. - DNS/service reachability: verify Service DNS names resolve from an in-cluster ephemeral pod.

Post-install validation (new `installer validate observability` stage, automatically invoked after successful install when `observability.validation.enabled=true`): - Prometheus: query `/api/v1/targets` and confirm expected jobs are present and up (gateway, control plane services, runtime/serving pods). If Prometheus is not installed/managed by Redwood, validation can be pointed at the customer Prometheus URL (or skipped). - Grafana: verify datasource exists and is healthy; confirm Redwood dashboards are present (by UID prefix) and have non-empty panel queries (basic check). - Output: installer prints a short “what we checked / what failed / what to do next” summary and writes a JSON artifact to the installer workdir for Support attachments.

## Configuration / flags - `observability.preflight.enabled` (default: true when `observability.enabled=true`) - `observability.preflight.mode` = `warn` | `block` (default: `warn`; we block only on missing StorageClass / unbound PVC prereqs when the installer is responsible for provisioning them) - `observability.validation.enabled` (default: true) - `observability.validation.prometheus.url` (optional; for customer-managed Prometheus) - `observability.validation.grafana.url` (optional; for customer-managed Grafana) - `observability.validation.timeoutSeconds` (default: 120)

## Implementation notes - Adds a small in-cluster “validator” Job (`redwood-observability-validator`) that runs with a minimal serviceAccount and uses K8s DNS to reach Prometheus/Grafana Services; avoids requiring the installer host to have network access into the cluster. - Prometheus checks are tolerant to slow startup: we poll targets until timeout and show the set of down targets with `lastError` where available. - Grafana checks use the internal service endpoint + admin creds from the existing secret (when Grafana is installer-managed). For customer-managed Grafana, we require an API token via values and skip dashboard validation if not provided.

## Security / privacy - No request payloads are logged. Validator output is limited to: target/job names, scrape health, HTTP status codes, and high-level errors. - Secrets are not printed; we redact URLs containing credentials and avoid dumping K8s Secret contents. - Added allowlist validation for external URLs to reduce accidental SSRF from misconfiguration.

## Tests - Unit tests for preflight rule evaluation + formatter for failure messages. - Integration test in CI: spins up kind cluster, installs with observability enabled, applies a restrictive NetworkPolicy to reproduce scrape failure, and asserts installer reports the expected actionable message (ENG-19156). - Manual: validated on an internal VPC reference cluster + simulated “customer-managed Prometheus” mode.

## Screenshots / example output (text) - Preflight warn example: `WARN observability/networkpolicy: NetworkPolicies detected in namespace redwood-private; Prometheus may be unable to scrape gateway:9090/metrics. Suggested fix: allow ingress from prometheus to pods with label app=redwood-gateway on port 9090.` - Post-install fail example: `FAIL observability/targets: 3/12 scrape targets down (redwood-runtime, redwood-router, redwood-policy). First error: context deadline exceeded. Suggested fix: verify NetworkPolicy allows prometheus->pods and that metrics port is exposed.`

## Rollout plan - Land behind defaults that are safe: warn-only preflight, post-install validation on by default but non-blocking (installer exit code remains 0; we surface a “validation failed” summary and write artifact). - After a couple of releases + noise review, consider switching specific checks to block in “supported minimum pack” mode.

## Related - Linear: ENG-19153 (preflight), ENG-19156 (smoke tests), PM-842 (minimum pack definition) - Support: SUP-49210 (missing dashboards after install), INT-11804 (CI failing smoke tests)

## Checklist - [x] Adds preflight checks for storage/network/resources - [x] Adds post-install validation for Prometheus scrapes and Grafana dashboards - [x] Safe logging / redaction - [x] CI coverage + kind-based integration test - [x] Docs stub added to installer `--help` + values schema

---

### Commits (squash summary) 1. `installer: add observability preflight framework + rules` 2. `installer: add in-cluster observability validator job` 3. `installer: implement prometheus targets validation` 4. `installer: implement grafana datasource + dashboard validation` 5. `ci: add kind test for networkpolicy scrape failures` 6. `docs: expose new observability preflight/validation flags`

### Changed areas / files (high level) - `cmd/redwood-private-installer/main.go` (wire new stages + flags) - `pkg/preflight/observability/*.go` (storage/network/resources rules) - `pkg/validate/observability/*.go` (prometheus/grafana validators) - `charts/private-installer/templates/validator-job.yaml` (new Job + RBAC) - `charts/private-installer/values.yaml` + `values.schema.json` (new config) - `ci/kind/private_observability_validation_test.sh` (integration coverage)

---

## Review conversation (highlights) **Allison Grant (requested changes):** The network preflight reads like it will block installs when NetworkPolicies exist. We need to be explicit that this is warn-only by default and avoid false positives (some customers pre-allow via namespace selectors). Also please align wording with our alert severity taxonomy (warn/block). **Diego Santos (author):** Updated defaults to `mode=warn`, added a note that we only block on definite provisioning prerequisites (StorageClass/PVC binding) when installer-managed. Also adjusted messaging to “detected NetworkPolicies; cannot determine allow rules reliably; here are common required allowances” and linked to the runbook stub.

**Rafael Mendes:** Post-install validation should not fail the whole install. Support wants an artifact they can ask customers for. Can we always write a JSON summary? **Diego Santos:** Yep — added `observability-validation-report.json` written to the installer workdir + referenced in stdout. Installer exits 0 unless core install failed.

**Marcus Lin:** Please ensure we don’t allow arbitrary URLs for customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana without guardrails (SSRF risk if installer runs with elevated network access). **Diego Santos:** Added URL allowlist (scheme must be http/https, no userinfo, no link-local IPs, no RFC1918 bypass unless explicitly allowed via `observability.validation.allowPrivateCIDRs=true`). Also validation is in-cluster by default so blast radius is limited to the cluster network.

**Logan Wright:** Target matching: relying on job names might drift as we refactor metrics. Prefer label-based matching or a small set of stable job names. **Diego Santos:** Switched to a two-step approach: (1) assert Prometheus is reachable and has any targets, (2) check that targets exist for a stable list of `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=redwood-private` scrape endpoints, and treat missing component targets as WARN unless `supportedMinimum=true`.

**Dylan Brooks:** Grafana dashboard validation shouldn’t require panel evaluation; it’s expensive and fragile. Just confirm dashboards are present + datasource is healthy. **Diego Santos:** Agreed; reduced panel query checks to a lightweight “dashboard JSON contains datasource ref” sanity check; removed render/panel eval.

Approvals: - Allison Grant: Approved after messaging/defaults changes - Rafael Mendes: Approved (requested artifact + non-blocking) - Marcus Lin: Approved after SSRF guardrails - Dylan Brooks: Approved

---

## CI - `build` ✅ - `unit-tests` ✅ - `lint` ✅ - `kind-private-install` ✅ - `kind-private-observability-validation` ✅ (added)

Merge: Squash merged by Diego Santos into `main`.

In hosted environments we have stronger environmental guarantees; in Private, the installer should surface actionable guidance before and immediately after install.

## What’s included Preflight checks (new `installer preflight observability` stage, also runs as part of `install` when enabled): - Storage: confirm a writable StorageClass exists (or user supplied `observability.storageClassName`), validate PVC access mode compatibility, validate requested sizes are >= minimums. - Resources: sanity check requests/limits for Prometheus + Grafana + log collector against node allocatable (best-effort; warns if cluster appears undersized). - Networking: detect whether NetworkPolicies are present in the target namespace and (if enabled) validate egress/ingress needed for Prometheus to scrape Redwood services (TCP/metrics ports) and for Grafana to reach Prometheus datasource. - DNS/service reachability: verify Service DNS names resolve from an in-cluster ephemeral pod.

Post-install validation (new `installer validate observability` stage, automatically invoked after successful install when `observability.validation.enabled=true`): - Prometheus: query `/api/v1/targets` and confirm expected jobs are present and up (gateway, control plane services, runtime/serving pods). If Prometheus is not installed/managed by Redwood, validation can be pointed at the customer Prometheus URL (or skipped). - Grafana: verify datasource exists and is healthy; confirm Redwood dashboards are present (by UID prefix) and have non-empty panel queries (basic check). - Output: installer prints a short “what we checked / what failed / what to do next” summary and writes a JSON artifact to the installer workdir for Support attachments.

## Configuration / flags - `observability.preflight.enabled` (default: true when `observability.enabled=true`) - `observability.preflight.mode` = `warn` | `block` (default: `warn`; we block only on missing StorageClass / unbound PVC prereqs when the installer is responsible for provisioning them) - `observability.validation.enabled` (default: true) - `observability.validation.prometheus.url` (optional; for customer-managed Prometheus) - `observability.validation.grafana.url` (optional; for customer-managed Grafana) - `observability.validation.timeoutSeconds` (default: 120)

## Implementation notes - Adds a small in-cluster “validator” Job (`redwood-observability-validator`) that runs with a minimal serviceAccount and uses K8s DNS to reach Prometheus/Grafana Services; avoids requiring the installer host to have network access into the cluster. - Prometheus checks are tolerant to slow startup: we poll targets until timeout and show the set of down targets with `lastError` where available. - Grafana checks use the internal service endpoint + admin creds from the existing secret (when Grafana is installer-managed). For customer-managed Grafana, we require an API token via values and skip dashboard validation if not provided.

## Security / privacy - No request payloads are logged. Validator output is limited to: target/job names, scrape health, HTTP status codes, and high-level errors. - Secrets are not printed; we redact URLs containing credentials and avoid dumping K8s Secret contents. - Added allowlist validation for external URLs to reduce accidental SSRF from misconfiguration.

## Tests - Unit tests for preflight rule evaluation + formatter for failure messages. - Integration test in CI: spins up kind cluster, installs with observability enabled, applies a restrictive NetworkPolicy to reproduce scrape failure, and asserts installer reports the expected actionable message (ENG-19156). - Manual: validated on an internal VPC reference cluster + simulated “customer-managed Prometheus” mode.

## Screenshots / example output (text) - Preflight warn example: `WARN observability/networkpolicy: NetworkPolicies detected in namespace redwood-private; Prometheus may be unable to scrape gateway:9090/metrics. Suggested fix: allow ingress from prometheus to pods with label app=redwood-gateway on port 9090.` - Post-install fail example: `FAIL observability/targets: 3/12 scrape targets down (redwood-runtime, redwood-router, redwood-policy). First error: context deadline exceeded. Suggested fix: verify NetworkPolicy allows prometheus->pods and that metrics port is exposed.`

## Rollout plan - Land behind defaults that are safe: warn-only preflight, post-install validation on by default but non-blocking (installer exit code remains 0; we surface a “validation failed” summary and write artifact). - After a couple of releases + noise review, consider switching specific checks to block in “supported minimum pack” mode.

## Related - Linear: ENG-19153 (preflight), ENG-19156 (smoke tests), PM-842 (minimum pack definition) - Support: SUP-49210 (missing dashboards after install), INT-11804 (CI failing smoke tests)

## Checklist - [x] Adds preflight checks for storage/network/resources - [x] Adds post-install validation for Prometheus scrapes and Grafana dashboards - [x] Safe logging / redaction - [x] CI coverage + kind-based integration test - [x] Docs stub added to installer `--help` + values schema

---

### Commits (squash summary) 1. `installer: add observability preflight framework + rules` 2. `installer: add in-cluster observability validator job` 3. `installer: implement prometheus targets validation` 4. `installer: implement grafana datasource + dashboard validation` 5. `ci: add kind test for networkpolicy scrape failures` 6. `docs: expose new observability preflight/validation flags`

### Changed areas / files (high level) - `cmd/redwood-private-installer/main.go` (wire new stages + flags) - `pkg/preflight/observability/*.go` (storage/network/resources rules) - `pkg/validate/observability/*.go` (prometheus/grafana validators) - `charts/private-installer/templates/validator-job.yaml` (new Job + RBAC) - `charts/private-installer/values.yaml` + `values.schema.json` (new config) - `ci/kind/private_observability_validation_test.sh` (integration coverage)

---

## Review conversation (highlights) **Allison Grant (requested changes):** The network preflight reads like it will block installs when NetworkPolicies exist. We need to be explicit that this is warn-only by default and avoid false positives (some customers pre-allow via namespace selectors). Also please align wording with our alert severity taxonomy (warn/block). **Diego Santos (author):** Updated defaults to `mode=warn`, added a note that we only block on definite provisioning prerequisites (StorageClass/PVC binding) when installer-managed. Also adjusted messaging to “detected NetworkPolicies; cannot determine allow rules reliably; here are common required allowances” and linked to the runbook stub.

**Rafael Mendes:** Post-install validation should not fail the whole install. Support wants an artifact they can ask customers for. Can we always write a JSON summary? **Diego Santos:** Yep — added `observability-validation-report.json` written to the installer workdir + referenced in stdout. Installer exits 0 unless core install failed.

**Marcus Lin:** Please ensure we don’t allow arbitrary URLs for customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana without guardrails (SSRF risk if installer runs with elevated network access). **Diego Santos:** Added URL allowlist (scheme must be http/https, no userinfo, no link-local IPs, no RFC1918 bypass unless explicitly allowed via `observability.validation.allowPrivateCIDRs=true`). Also validation is in-cluster by default so blast radius is limited to the cluster network.

**Logan Wright:** Target matching: relying on job names might drift as we refactor metrics. Prefer label-based matching or a small set of stable job names. **Diego Santos:** Switched to a two-step approach: (1) assert Prometheus is reachable and has any targets, (2) check that targets exist for a stable list of `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=redwood-private` scrape endpoints, and treat missing component targets as WARN unless `supportedMinimum=true`.

**Dylan Brooks:** Grafana dashboard validation shouldn’t require panel evaluation; it’s expensive and fragile. Just confirm dashboards are present + datasource is healthy. **Diego Santos:** Agreed; reduced panel query checks to a lightweight “dashboard JSON contains datasource ref” sanity check; removed render/panel eval.

Approvals: - Allison Grant: Approved after messaging/defaults changes - Rafael Mendes: Approved (requested artifact + non-blocking) - Marcus Lin: Approved after SSRF guardrails - Dylan Brooks: Approved

---

## CI - `build` ✅ - `unit-tests` ✅ - `lint` ✅ - `kind-private-install` ✅ - `kind-private-observability-validation` ✅ (added)

Merge: Squash merged by Diego Santos into `main`.

title: Include offline observability artifacts in air-gapped bundle + update manifest
preview: Context: Several on-prem / air-gapped Private installs reported Grafana coming up without any Redwood dashboards after install/upgrade because the offline bundle did not include the minimum-pack dashboard JSONs and Prometheus rules (SUP-49210, Slack thread: airgap-bundle-missing-grafana-dashboards). This PR updates the air-gap bundle build so the observability artifacts are shipped as first-class, versioned bundle contents. What changed: (1) add observability artifacts to the airgap bundle payload: Grafana dashboard bundle (runtime + control plane), Prometheus alerting/recording rules, and default log collector configs; (2) update the bundle manifest schema usage to explicitly list these artifacts under the 'artifacts' section with stable filenames and SHA256s; (3) wire extraction/install into post-unpack steps so upgrades can safely rehydrate dashboards/rules without requiring outbound fetches; (4) add a lightweight validation step in the bundle build pipeline to fail if any referenced observability artifact path is missing. Notes/constraints: No customer data is embedded; artifacts are static JSON/YAML shipped with the installer. This does not enable/disable observability components by default; it only ensures the offline bundle contains the required files when customers enable the minimum pack via Helm values. Backward compatibility: Existing bundle consumers continue to work; new manifest entries are additive. Follow-ups: noise tuning and runbook links live in the observability-pack rules PRs; this PR only handles packaging and offline delivery.

title: Add installer docs for Terraform VPC module integration + improve network preflight errors
preview:
## Why
During design partner pilots we keep losing time to “is this VPC wired correctly?” ambiguity. We now have an alpha set of Terraform VPC modules in `redwood-terraform`, but the installer docs do not explain:
- what outputs the installer expects / how to map module outputs to installer config
- which VPC endpoints + DNS settings are required for private-only installs
- how to interpret common failure modes (image pull failures, intermittent DNS)

This PR adds an explicit integration doc page in the installer repo, links out to the Terraform module suite, and tightens up a couple of preflight error messages so that support/SE can triage faster.

## What changed
### Docs
- Added a new guide: `docs/terraform-vpc-modules-integration.md`
  - Minimal “happy path” example wiring Terraform outputs into the installer config
  - Required endpoints list for fully-private installs (no public egress): ECR (api + dkr), S3 (gateway), STS, CloudWatch Logs (if log shipping), EC2 (for some bootstrap flows), KMS (if using CMKs), and optional endpoints called out
  - DNS notes: Private DNS behavior for interface endpoints, VPC resolver settings, and the most common pitfall (endpoint exists but private DNS disabled)
  - Connectivity notes: placeholders for VPN/DX; explicitly out-of-scope for alpha to auto-configure
- Updated `README.md` and `docs/troubleshooting.md` to link to the new guide and call out the alpha status + version pinning recommendation for modules.

### Installer messaging
- Updated the missing-endpoint preflight error copy to include:
  - the exact AWS endpoint(s) we detected as missing
  - the common remediation (“enable interface endpoint + enable Private DNS”)
  - a link to the new docs page
- Added a troubleshooting hint for intermittent DNS resolution that points to VPC resolver / DHCP options + CoreDNS forwarders.

## How to test
1) `make docs-lint` (markdown + link checker)
2) Run installer preflight against a VPC with ECR endpoints intentionally missing:
   - Expect: preflight fails with actionable error + docs link
3) Run preflight against a VPC with ECR endpoints present but Private DNS disabled:
   - Expect: preflight warns/fails with “endpoint present but Private DNS disabled” guidance (best-effort detection).

## Notes / scope
- This does not change the underlying endpoint detection logic (that’s in #19110); this PR focuses on documentation and making the failure text actionable.
- We intentionally do not prescribe a single subnet topology beyond “private subnets with route to NAT or endpoints”; the reference Terraform modules are the opinionated path.

## References
- SUP-28411 (installer fails pulling images due to missing ECR endpoints / Private DNS)
- SUP-28458 (intermittent DNS in private subnets during pilot)
- Linear ENG-4869 (align installer preflight checks with VPC module defaults)

## Checklist
- [x] Docs labeled as alpha and link to module repo + release notes
- [x] Added troubleshooting section for endpoints + DNS
- [x] Ran docs lint/link check in CI
- [x] Reviewed by Infra/Networking + Security

title: Installer: detect required VPC endpoints + warn on missing ECR/private DNS
preview:
## Summary
This adds a network-focused preflight step to the Redwood Private installer to catch the most common failure mode we’ve seen in VPC-based pilots: image pulls failing because required interface endpoints (ECR API/DKR) are missing and/or the endpoint is present but **Private DNS is disabled**, causing the node to resolve the public ECR hostname.

The preflight is intentionally defensive: it attempts AWS-based detection when IAM permissions allow it, and otherwise falls back to DNS + connectivity heuristics. In both cases it produces targeted, copy/paste-able remediation guidance and links to the Terraform module docs.

## Motivation / Context
- Support + SE feedback shows we’re burning cycles late in the install when kubelet/containerd can’t pull images.
- In private subnet deployments with restricted egress, missing ECR endpoints is fatal.
- Even when endpoints exist, we’ve seen pilots where ECR endpoints were created with `private_dns_enabled = false`, resulting in confusing behavior.

Refs:
- Jira: SUP-28411 (installer fails pulling images; missing ECR endpoints / private DNS)
- Linear: ENG-4869

## What this changes
### New preflight checks
- **ECR interface endpoints**: validates presence of both:
  - `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.api`
  - `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.dkr`
- **Private DNS on ECR endpoints**: if endpoints are detected via AWS APIs, warns when `PrivateDnsEnabled` is false.
- **Fallback DNS heuristic** (when installer cannot call `ec2:DescribeVpcEndpoints`): attempts to resolve `api.ecr.${region}.amazonaws.com` and flags likely public resolution when:
  - we resolve to public IP space (best-effort check), or
  - we can’t resolve at all from the installer environment.
- **Actionable messaging**:
  - Explains exactly which endpoints are required
  - Calls out private DNS explicitly
  - Includes “if you’re using the alpha Terraform modules” guidance + expected module knobs

### Flags / behavior
- Adds `--skip-network-preflight` for advanced customers who want to bypass this (still defaults to running).
- Adds `--preflight-verbose` to print additional context (e.g., which detection path was used).
- If AWS API calls are blocked by IAM, we **do not fail**; we emit a warning and continue with the DNS heuristic. This keeps the installer usable with strict permission boundaries.

### Docs
- Updates troubleshooting docs to include:
  - how to verify endpoints from AWS console/CLI
  - what “Private DNS enabled” means in practice
  - typical symptoms in k8s events when ECR is misconfigured

## Implementation details
- Added `internal/preflight/network.go` for the new checks, wired into the existing preflight runner.
- Added a small AWS helper `internal/aws/vpc_endpoints.go` (uses EC2 DescribeVpcEndpoints when available).
- The preflight tries, in order:
  1) detect region
  2) attempt AWS endpoint discovery (requires `ec2:DescribeVpcEndpoints`, `ec2:DescribeSubnets` only)
  3) fall back to DNS resolution checks
- Errors are categorized as:
  - `WARN` when detection is incomplete / cannot confirm
  - `FAIL` only when we have high confidence that install will not succeed (e.g., DNS completely broken for critical hostnames)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - endpoint name matching per region
  - messaging for missing api vs dkr endpoint
  - private DNS disabled warning path
  - IAM access denied -> fallback path
- Manual validation:
  - sandbox VPC with endpoints present (private DNS enabled) -> clean preflight
  - sandbox VPC missing ECR endpoints -> warning + remediation
  - sandbox VPC endpoints present but private DNS disabled -> warning with explicit callout

## Screenshots / output examples
Example warning:
- "ECR endpoints not detected: com.amazonaws.us-west-2.ecr.api, com.amazonaws.us-west-2.ecr.dkr"
- "If using the Redwood VPC Terraform modules (alpha), enable the endpoints module and ensure private_dns_enabled = true for ECR"

## Follow-ups
- Once the Terraform modules alpha lands broadly, link directly to the exact module README section + anchor.
- Consider adding optional checks for S3 Gateway endpoint / CloudWatch Logs endpoint (not required for image pulls but common in fully private deployments).

title: Verify air-gapped upgrade bundles via SHA256 manifest + signature; improve missing/incorrect artifact errors
preview: Context: Design partners and Support have hit confusing failures in the offline/air-gapped upgrade flow when bundles are incomplete, corrupted, or built against the wrong target version (see SUP-18510). Today the installer will often fail late (during image/chart pull) and the error doesn’t clearly tell the operator what’s missing or mismatched. This PR adds deterministic bundle verification up front: (1) a required SHA256 checksum manifest for all bundle artifacts, (2) optional signature verification for the manifest, and (3) clearer, actionable error messages that enumerate missing/incorrect artifacts before any cluster mutations. Behavior: - New required file inside bundle: bundle.manifest.json (list of artifacts + expected sha256 + size + logical type). - New required file inside bundle: bundle.manifest.sha256 (sha256sum of bundle.manifest.json) OR embedded sha256 in the manifest (we accept either; prefer sha256 file). - Optional files: bundle.manifest.sig + bundle.manifest.pub (ed25519). If present, installer verifies signature and prints signer fingerprint. (Signature is optional for this release to avoid breaking existing customers; we will gate to required in a future major). - Installer runs verification during upgrade plan generation and again immediately before apply (defense-in-depth if bundle is swapped between plan/apply). - Error messaging: missing artifact(s) are printed as a numbered list with expected paths; checksum mismatch prints expected vs actual sha256 and suggests re-download/re-mirror; version mismatch prints bundle target version vs requested version and points to the compatibility policy doc. - Adds break-glass flag: --skip-bundle-verification (hidden behind --i-understand-the-risks) for Support-only workflows. Security notes: No secrets are stored in the bundle verification metadata. Public key is optional and can be provided via file path or embedded in the bundle. Logging: verification results are included in the installer run summary and emitted as a structured event (bundle_verify_started / bundle_verify_failed / bundle_verify_succeeded). Testing: unit tests cover manifest parsing, sha validation, signature verification, and error formatting; integration test adds a corrupted bundle fixture and asserts failure before any k8s writes. Rollout: This is backward compatible for existing bundles (signature optional; checksum manifest required only when --airgapped-bundle is used). For online installs nothing changes. Checklist: - [x] Verify missing chart/image entries are surfaced preflight - [x] Verify corrupted tarball errors are actionable - [x] Add docs snippet to installer help output - [x] Add integration fixture + CI lane

title: Add upgrade rollback command + persisted state machine
preview:
Implements `redwood-private upgrade rollback` backed by a persisted upgrade state machine. The rollback command reads the last recorded upgrade attempt (plan + execution state), runs rollback hooks in a deterministic order (control plane first, then data plane), and verifies the cluster is healthy after rollback. This is intended to make rollback an operator-safe, auditable action rather than a collection of manual kubectl/helm steps.

Context
- Follows PR #4821 (upgrade plan generator) and PR #4850 (backup orchestration + snapshot hooks).
- Designed for N-1 -> N upgrades with explicit rollback entry points for failures during plan execution.
- Addresses support incidents where upgrades failed during DB migrations and operators needed a consistent rollback procedure (SUP-18421, SUP-18488).

Operator UX
- Roll back the last attempted upgrade: `redwood-private upgrade rollback`
- Roll back and force a specific target revision from stored history: `redwood-private upgrade rollback --target-revision <revision-id>`
- Dry-run the rollback (prints steps + would-run hooks): `redwood-private upgrade rollback --dry-run`
- Status summary including rollback eligibility: `redwood-private upgrade status`

What gets persisted
- Writes an UpgradeExecutionState artifact at `${STATE_DIR}/upgrade/state.json` (defaults to `/var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade/state.json`) and mirrors a summarized view to a Kubernetes ConfigMap (`redwood-private-upgrade-state`) for support bundles.
- Tracks: plan_id, from_version, to_version, stage, step, timestamps, backup refs, helm releases touched, and rollback eligibility.

Rollback semantics (high-level)
- Rollback is allowed when: (a) an upgrade execution state exists, (b) the recorded attempt has not been finalized as success, and (c) rollback hooks are available for the stages that completed.
- Rollback ordering:
  1) Pre-rollback checks (cluster access, namespace existence, artifact availability for the target version)
  2) Control-plane rollback (helm release rollback / apply pinned chart version)
  3) Data-plane rollback (roll back serving components only after CP confirms ready)
  4) Restore data if needed (optional; only when the plan recorded a backup and migration stage completed)
  5) Post-rollback verification (subset of prechecks + readiness gates)
- Idempotency: rollback steps are safe to re-run; the state machine records completed rollback steps and will skip already-completed steps unless `--force-step` is used.

Post-rollback health verification
- Waits for Deployments/StatefulSets to become Ready (with timeouts and actionable failure messages)
- Verifies API health endpoints (installer talks to the in-cluster CP service when available)
- Runs a minimal preflight suite: DB connectivity, schema version compatibility, and control-plane -> data-plane connectivity
- Emits a rollback summary with next actions when verification fails (collect support bundle; recommended kubectl commands)

Auditability
- Writes a structured rollback report at `${STATE_DIR}/upgrade/rollback-report.json` including executed steps, hook outputs, and verification results.
- Adds support-bundle section `private-upgrade/` with state.json, rollback-report.json, and last 2 installer logs.

Test plan
- Unit tests for state transitions (happy path + partial failure cases)
- Integration tests in kind for:
  - failure mid-control-plane stage -> rollback
  - failure post-migration stage -> rollback with restore option
  - rollback idempotency (rerun rollback)
- Manual (staging): forced failure by injecting invalid image ref during upgrade, then rollback.

- [x] Rollback command supports dry-run
- [x] Hooks have stable contract + timeouts
- [x] Post-rollback health verification
- [x] Docs snippet in `docs/commands.md` (follow-up: full runbook in Confluence)

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Emit platform audit log events from control plane for rollback start/stop (tracked in monorepo)
- Expand restore verification (application-level smoke tests) in e2e suite
