title: Private upgrades: backup orchestrator + snapshot hooks (CSI/EBS) + optional logical backup
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-class backup orchestration phase to the Private upgrade workflow. The installer now runs a pre-upgrade backup step that can (a) create volume snapshots via pluggable snapshotters (initial support: Kubernetes CSI VolumeSnapshot and AWS EBS snapshots via CSI annotations) and/or (b) perform an optional logical DB backup (pg_dump) when snapshots are unavailable or when operators request an additional safety net. The orchestrator emits structured progress events (for CLI + future UI) and persists backup metadata into the upgrade state store so rollback and support bundles can reference what happened. Motivation: We’ve had multiple upgrade incidents/near-misses where rollback was risky because there was no deterministic backup step or because snapshot behaviors differed across environments. This change makes upgrades safe-by-default and reduces operator guesswork, aligning with ENG-4838 and the broader “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” project. Design/behavior: 1) New phase in plan execution: pre_upgrade_backup runs after prechecks and before any control-plane schema migrations or chart changes. 2) Backup strategies: SnapshotBackup (preferred) creates snapshots for PVCs selected by label/allowlist (db, control-plane state, queue/cache if configured). Uses CSI VolumeSnapshot API if supported; falls back to an AWS EBS snapshot flow when running on EKS with the EBS CSI driver (detected via StorageClass provisioner + PV topology). LogicalBackup (optional) runs a job in-cluster to produce a compressed pg_dump stored to a configured object store endpoint or a local path in on-prem mode. The logical backup is explicitly opt-in (--backup.logical.enabled) and requires credentials/target to be present. 3) Encryption and access: The orchestrator enforces encryption expectations when possible (e.g., checks StorageClass parameters / snapshot class annotations). For logical backup targets, it validates that the destination is configured for server-side encryption when supported (S3 SSE-S3/SSE-KMS) and warns otherwise (non-blocking). 4) Progress reporting: Standardized events are emitted for start/progress/complete/failure with stable step IDs. CLI prints a compact progress view; verbose mode includes per-volume timing and snapshot IDs. 5) Failure handling: Backup failures are blocking by default (upgrade aborts before any mutations). Partial snapshot creation is treated as failure; the output includes the created snapshot IDs to help cleanup. A --backup.continue-on-failure flag exists for break-glass situations (hidden; requires confirmation prompt). 6) Auditability: The upgrade state store now records backup artifacts (snapshot IDs, VolumeSnapshot names, logical backup URI, timestamps, duration, and validation status). This is used by future rollback commands and included in support bundles (ENG-4899 follow-up will wire the bundle). Operator UX: New installer flags: --backup.enabled (default true for upgrade), --backup.strategy=snapshot|logical|snapshot+logical, --backup.snapshot.pvcSelector (label selector), --backup.logical.enabled, --backup.logical.destination (s3://… or file:///…), --backup.timeout, --backup.continue-on-failure (hidden). New commands: redwood-private upgrade plan now includes a Backup stage when enabled; redwood-private upgrade run prints a Backup section with progress. Notably, this PR does not implement restore; restore/rollback hooks will consume the recorded artifacts (ENG-4844 / PR-4862). Testing: Adds unit tests for snapshot selection, CSI capability detection, and state persistence. Adds an integration test job in CI that runs a mocked snapshotter and verifies that upgrade plan execution halts on backup failure. Limitations: On-prem snapshot support depends on the cluster’s CSI driver exposing VolumeSnapshot. If VolumeSnapshot CRDs are missing, snapshot backups will fail with a clear remediation message (install snapshot-controller + CRDs) and operators can choose logical backups instead. Logical backup currently supports Postgres only (our control-plane DB). Follow-ups: (1) Add restore hooks + verification checks for snapshots and logical backups (ENG-4838). (2) Support additional snapshot backends and explicit SnapshotClass selection (requested by design partners). (3) Wire backup metadata into support bundle and audit log events (ENG-4899 + redwood monorepo PR for audit logging).

title: Private upgrades: add upgrade plan generator (staged plan + artifact validation + auditable summary)
preview: Summary This PR introduces an upgrade plan generator to `redwood-private-installer` as the first building block for the Private upgrade + rollback mechanism. The generator produces a staged plan (control-plane first, then data-plane), validates required artifacts before any changes are made, and writes an auditable plan artifact + human-readable summary to disk. Motivation/Context We need a safe-by-default workflow for Redwood Private upgrades (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) with deterministic execution, clear failure modes, and artifacts that can be attached to support bundles and audit logs. The plan generator is intentionally separated from execution so operators (and Release Eng/SRE) can review what will happen before they run it. This aligns with ENG-4821 and the rollout UX direction in DES-311. What’s included - New CLI: `redwood-private upgrade plan` - Produces a versioned plan artifact (schema v1) with stable stage/step IDs. - Stages are ordered and explicit (preflight placeholder, artifact validation, backup placeholder, control-plane rollout placeholder, data-plane rollout placeholder, post-verify placeholder). Execution wiring will come in follow-up PRs; this PR focuses on plan generation and validation. - Artifact validation (fail-fast) - Validates the presence and resolvability of required artifacts for the target version: Helm chart references, required container images (by digest when available), and (when `--airgapped-bundle` is provided) bundle manifest presence and checksum file presence. Signature verification is intentionally out of scope here and will follow in PR-4871. - Produces actionable error messages including the specific missing artifact and the remediation hint (e.g., “configure --artifact-mirror” / “rebuild bundle” / “check registry connectivity”). - Auditable outputs - Writes the plan JSON and a summary text file under `${STATE_DIR}/upgrade-plans/<timestamp>-<from>-to-<to>/`. - Captures: planner version, schema version, CLI args (redacted), input digests (cluster ID, target version, artifact source), and a deterministic plan hash. - Summary includes a step table with stage IDs, blocking validations, and estimated operator prompts. Operator UX Example `redwood-private upgrade plan --from 1.18.2 --to 1.19.0 --artifact-source registry --state-dir /var/lib/redwood-private` Output (abridged): - Plan written: /var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade-plans/2025-04-24T18-22-10Z-1.18.2-to-1.19.0/plan.json - Summary written: /var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade-plans/2025-04-24T18-22-10Z-1.18.2-to-1.19.0/summary.txt - Plan hash: sha256:7b1c… Stages: - validate.artifacts (blocking) - backup.capture (blocking; placeholder until ENG-4838) - rollout.control_plane (blocking; placeholder until chart phase split lands) - rollout.data_plane (blocking; placeholder) - verify.post_upgrade (blocking; placeholder) Notes on determinism and safety - Stage and step IDs are stable across repeated runs for the same inputs; the only non-deterministic value is the output directory timestamp. The plan hash is computed over a canonical JSON form that excludes the timestamped path. - The plan generator does not attempt cluster mutations. It only reads config and validates artifact accessibility (network reachability checks are not included here; those will be part of preflight checks in PR-4834). Tests - Added unit tests for: - schema serialization and canonicalization - stable plan IDs across runs - artifact validation error surfaces - Golden plan fixtures for a handful of common configs (online registry, mirror, air-gapped bundle). Follow-ups (not in this PR) - Preflight check framework + checks (ENG-4829 / PR-4834) - Backup orchestration + snapshot hooks (ENG-4838 / PR-4850) - Rollback command and state machine (ENG-4844 / PR-4862) - Bundle signature verification + stricter offline validation (PR-4871) - Version compatibility gating integration (redwood PR-29104) Review notes - Please focus review on: plan schema shape, determinism, error messages/remediation hints, and state-dir file layout. - The plan schema is intentionally minimal and forward-compatible; we can extend with rollback hooks and migration markers once execution exists. Reviewer discussion highlights - Connor O’Brien: Requested schema version field and explicit “blocking” attribute per stage for change-management gating; addressed in commit “planner: schema_v1”. - Sean Gallagher: Asked for plan artifact redaction to avoid leaking registry creds/headers; CLI arg capture now redacts values for `--registry-password`, `--token`, and any env var expansion with `*_TOKEN`. - Kenji Nakamura: Requested summary output to be operator-first (step table + next actions) rather than raw JSON dump; summary formatter added and default output now includes a short table. - Sahana Iyer: Flagged a flaky golden test on Windows path separators in CI; normalized path rendering in tests and re-ran CI successfully. Merge outcome Squash-merged to main after approvals (Connor O’Brien, Sean Gallagher) and CI green. No runtime behavior changes until the execution path is wired; this is a new command and new internal planner package.

title: Implement pluggable preflight check framework for Private upgrades
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a pluggable preflight check framework in the Private installer. The framework standardizes check execution, result reporting, severity levels, and remediation pointers so upgrades are safer-by-default and failures are actionable for operators. Context: Part of the “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” effort. This PR focuses on the framework + CLI surface for preflight runs; follow-up PRs will add/expand the actual checks (cluster health, storage, RBAC/SSO reachability, artifact availability, etc.). Goals: (1) Make prechecks composable (registerable plugins) and deterministic, (2) Provide consistent UX for operators and Support, (3) Establish blocking vs warning semantics that can be used by upgrade plan generation and change-management gating. Design notes: Checks are registered by ID and grouped into named phases (environment, control-plane, data-plane, artifacts). Each check returns a CheckResult with: status (PASS/WARN/FAIL/SKIP), severity (BLOCKER/WARNING/INFO), a short summary, optional details, and remediation pointers (human-readable steps + optional doc links). The runner supports timeouts per-check, a global deadline, and produces both console output and a JSON artifact suitable for audit/support bundles. CLI: Adds `redwood-private preflight run` (alias wired for upgrade flows in subsequent PRs). Supports `--format=table|json`, `--output <path>`, `--fail-on-warning` (default false), `--include`/`--exclude` (by check ID or group), and `--timeout` controls. Output example (json): `{ "run_id": "pf_...", "installer_version": "0.19.0", "target_version": "private-1.42.0", "results": [ ... ], "summary": { "blockers": 0, "warnings": 2 } }` (full schema in `docs/preflight-result-schema.md`). Implementation details: - New package `internal/preflight/` with: `registry` (check registration + metadata), `runner` (execution + concurrency limits), and `report` (renderers: table/json). - Standardized error taxonomy for checks to map common failures into remediation hints (e.g., kube auth failure vs API server unreachable vs RBAC forbidden). - Basic built-in checks included as scaffolding/examples: Kubernetes API reachability, namespace write access, and installer artifact writeability (output dir). These are intentionally minimal; real upgrade checks land in ENG-4829 follow-ups. - Adds a persistent “last preflight” record under the installer state dir to be consumed by the upgrade planner (plan generator PR) and support bundles. - Logging: all checks emit structured logs with `check_id`, `severity`, and `status` fields; runner emits a single summary line for easy grep during on-call. Operator UX: Table output uses stable ordering and explicitly labels “BLOCKING” items; remediation text is capped in the table view with a `--verbose` option to print full remediation steps. Testing: Unit tests cover registration, include/exclude filtering, timeout handling, renderer output stability, and JSON schema validation. Integration test spins up a kind cluster and validates kube reachability + RBAC check behavior under simulated restricted RBAC. Checklist: - [x] Framework + registry + runner - [x] JSON + table renderers - [x] Minimal built-in checks as examples - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Docs: result schema + CLI usage - [ ] Add full precheck inventory (follow-up PRs under ENG-4829) Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity semantics (BLOCKER/WARNING/INFO) and defaults, (b) output stability (Support will consume this), and (c) security concerns around logging potentially sensitive info in remediation/details. Release notes: Adds new `preflight run` command and machine-readable preflight output artifact to support upgrade gating and troubleshooting.

title: Wire up RBAC v2 admin bootstrap for Private installs (initial role bindings + IdP group mapping docs)
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a safe, deterministic “admin bootstrap” path for RBAC v2 in Redwood Private installs to prevent lockout under deny-by-default. On first install/upgrade (when RBAC v2 is enabled), the installer will create an initial role binding granting a built-in admin role to a customer-specified subject (email or IdP group). This also documents the recommended approach for mapping IdP groups (SAML/OIDC) to Redwood roles in Private.

Context / Motivation: As we roll RBAC v2 into Private, new clusters can end up with RBAC enforcement enabled before any role bindings exist, resulting in a hard lockout where no identity has permissions to create the first role binding. Hosted/Dedicated can rely on internal provisioning workflows; Private needs an install-time “seed” that is explicit, auditable, and easy for customers to configure. This PR implements the minimum bootstrap mechanism (one initial binding + recovery tooling) and ties it to policy bundle versions so that it remains consistent across upgrades.

What’s included:
- Installer config/values to specify a bootstrap admin identity:
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email` (single user email)
  - OR `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` (IdP group string as emitted in claims)
  - Mutually exclusive; installer fails fast if both are set.
- Install/upgrade hook that (idempotently) creates:
  - RoleBinding: `bootstrap-admin` scoped to the org created by the installer (and cluster scope where required for installer-only operations)
  - Role: `org_admin` (reference to built-in template from the policy bundle; no ad-hoc permission list in installer)
  - Annotation: `rbac.redwood.ai/bootstrap=true` and `rbac.redwood.ai/policyBundleVersion=<version>` on the binding for evidence/debug.
- Explicit behavior gating:
  - No-op unless `RBAC_V2_ENABLED=true` (or Helm equivalent `rbacV2.enabled: true`).
  - If RBAC v2 is enabled but no bootstrap subject is provided, installer prints a blocking error with next steps (including recovery command) rather than proceeding.
- Recovery / break-fix command:
  - Adds `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --email <user>` and `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --idp-group <group>`.
  - Requires local cluster admin kubeconfig (i.e., this is a cluster-operator action) and emits an audit event `rbac.bootstrap_admin_binding.created`.
- Docs:
  - Adds a Private guide section: “Mapping IdP groups to Redwood RBAC roles” with examples for Okta (SAML), Azure AD (OIDC), and generic OIDC.
  - Documents recommended separation of duties: use a group-based binding for day-to-day admin, and keep a small break-glass user separate.

Implementation notes:
- Uses Control Plane RBAC v2 APIs when available; falls back to writing the RoleBinding CRD directly if the Control Plane is not yet reachable during install. Both paths converge on the same schema and labels.
- Idempotency: binding create is upsert-like. If the binding exists, installer verifies the subject and role match the requested config; if they differ, installer fails with a clear message to avoid silently escalating or breaking access.
- Scoping: bootstrap binding is created at org scope; additionally, for installer-required cluster-level operations (e.g., registering the cluster, health checks), a minimal `private_cluster_operator` role binding is created only when `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.includeClusterOps=true` is set. Default is false.
- Audit: for both installer and `rwctl` flows, we emit an audit log event containing subject type (user vs group), subject identifier, role template name, scope, and policy bundle version.

Config examples (Helm values):
- Email bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email: "security-admin@customer.com"`
- Group bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup: "Redwood-Org-Admins"`

Docs excerpt: For OIDC, ensure your IdP includes groups in the ID token or userinfo response and that the claim name configured in Redwood matches. Example: `oidc.groupClaim: "groups"` and then bind `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` to a string present in that claim.

Testing:
- Added an integration test that brings up a minimal Private control plane with RBAC v2 enabled and asserts:
  - Bootstrap RoleBinding is created.
  - A user with matching email can call `/v1/orgs/{id}/roles:list` (sanity check).
  - A non-matching identity receives `PERMISSION_DENIED`.
- Added unit tests for config validation, idempotent upsert behavior, and Control Plane unavailable fallback path.

Operational considerations:
- Upgrade path: If upgrading an existing cluster to RBAC v2, bootstrap binding is only created if no admin bindings exist (to avoid changing existing customer setups). Installer logs whether it created or skipped bootstrap.
- Evidence: The binding annotations and audit event are intended to be attachable in SOC2 evidence packets for “authorized administrators provisioned via documented process”.

Related:
- Linear: ENG-18410 (Private installer admin bootstrap), ENG-18388 (feature-flag rollout)
- Follow-up (not included): Add Console copy linking to Private docs for group claim troubleshooting; tracked in DES-927.

Checklist:
- [x] Idempotent bootstrap binding creation
- [x] Fast-fail on misconfiguration (both email and group)
- [x] Audit event emitted
- [x] Docs for IdP group mapping (SAML/OIDC)
- [x] Integration tests for fresh install

title: Private installer: pinning requirements for Runtime LTS artifacts (checksums, digests, rollback)
preview:
Context: This PR is primarily a requirements/alignment thread captured in the PR description + review comments to unblock implementation of Runtime LTS pinning in the Redwood Private installer. Dedicated/Private enterprise customers want: (1) the ability to pin a specific runtime patch (e.g., 1.18.1-lts) for 6–12+ months, (2) verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance) that can be audited, and (3) a predictable rollback path when a patch introduces regressions. This PR documents and finalizes the installer-facing requirements so Release Eng can publish the right metadata and Runtime can keep semantics stable across the LTS line.

Requirements agreed in this thread:
- Pin semantics (must be explicit):
  - Installer MUST support pinning to an exact LTS patch version string (e.g., runtime=1.18.1-lts) and MUST NOT silently upgrade within the LTS line.
  - Installer SHOULD optionally support pinning by immutable image digest (sha256:...) to satisfy strict change control.
  - If both version and digest are provided, digest wins and the installer records both for audit.
- Artifact verification (enterprise default):
  - Installer MUST download a release manifest for the selected patch containing: image tag, image digest, per-arch digests, sha256 checksums for auxiliary tarballs, provenance pointer, and release notes URL.
  - Installer MUST verify sha256 checksums of downloaded tarballs (air-gapped flow) and MUST verify the runtime image digest matches the manifest when pulling from registry.
  - Signature verification: in this phase we capture requirement + interface; implementation may be gated behind availability of signing keys/verification tooling (tracked in ENG-4832). The installer will accept a --verify-signatures flag (default on when signatures exist; fallback to checksum-only with a loud warning).
- Rollback (operator safe):
  - Installer MUST support rollback to the previously installed runtime patch (one-step rollback) and MUST preserve the previous runtime image reference (tag + digest) and manifest in state.
  - Rollback MUST be supported for both online registry pulls and air-gapped artifact bundles.
  - Rollback MUST not require re-running full cluster bootstrap; it should be a targeted runtime component rollback.
- Observability + audit:
  - Installer MUST write an install record capturing: selected channel, requested version, resolved version, resolved digests, manifest checksum, and timestamps.
  - Installer MUST surface a single command to print the current pinned version + digests for audit (used in SOC2/ISO evidence requests).
- UX + defaults:
  - Default behavior for Private is unchanged (track recommended stable), but when customers opt into LTS they should see an explicit prompt/flag path that makes the “no auto-upgrades” property clear.
  - For regulated customers, we expect most to use exact version pinning, with digest pinning as a stricter option.

Non-requirements explicitly called out:
- Not promising that Private installer can automatically select “the newest patch in the LTS line” (that conflicts with change control expectations).
- Not implementing full signature verification unless Release Eng artifacts are signed consistently; however, we are locking the installer interface now.

Implementation notes included here to keep scope tight:
- New config knobs will be added under runtime: channel (stable|lts), version, digest, verify_checksums, verify_signatures.
- Installer will fetch a small JSON manifest published alongside LTS releases (owned by Release Eng). For air-gapped, customers can supply the manifest file path.
- We record state in the existing installer state directory and store the previous runtime reference for rollback.

Why a PR for requirements: This was the fastest way to converge across Release Eng, Security, and Private packaging while we already had PR-663 open for the pinning feature work. The conversation below is the canonical alignment and is referenced in the follow-up implementation commits.

title: Wire retention defaults into Private install + document purge job backing services
preview:
Title: Wire retention defaults into Private install + document purge job backing services

Context
Private customers (regulated workloads) are asking two recurring questions during security reviews: (1) what are the default retention behaviors when they install Redwood Private, and (2) what infrastructure is required to reliably enforce purges (scheduled jobs, storage/indexing dependencies, and verification signals). This PR updates the private installer to ship private-friendly defaults for retention + audit logging and adds a dedicated doc section describing purge job prerequisites.

Scope
- Set secure retention defaults in the installer Helm values (aligned to retention controls v1): conservative request/response log retention, longer audit log retention, and explicit “disabled logging” behavior where applicable.
- Enable purge job runner + recommended throttles by default (idempotent, bounded deletes) with opt-out in values.
- Document required backing services for purge enforcement (what is mandatory vs recommended) and what to validate post-install (dashboards/metrics).
- Document upgrade notes (how to migrate from older installs that lack retention sections in values.yaml).

Non-goals
- This does not implement TTL enforcement itself (handled in redwood monorepo + telemetry stores).
- This does not add legal hold UI/flows (control plane feature).

Key decisions
- Defaults are “secure by default” for Private: request/response logs are short-lived unless explicitly extended; audit logs default to longer retention to satisfy auditability expectations.
- Purge jobs are enabled by default but throttled to avoid DB pressure. Operators can tune batch size / max runtime.
- Installer docs treat purge backing services as first-class dependencies: if you don’t deploy them, retention will degrade (policy is accepted but enforcement can lag/fail).

Defaults (Private)
- audit_logs: enabled, default TTL 365d (configurable)
- request_response_logs: enabled, default TTL 30d (configurable; can be set to 0 to disable storage)
- traces: default TTL 14d (if trace storage enabled)
- metrics: default TTL 30d (if long-term metrics storage enabled)
- artifacts (eval datasets/report packages): default TTL 90d when stored in object storage (if compliance pack enabled)

Implementation notes
- Adds a `retention:` section to the installer chart values with explicit per-class TTLs and a single place to switch purge jobs on/off.
- Adds `purgeJobs:` tuning (schedule, concurrency, maxRowsPerRun, maxRuntimeSeconds) with safe defaults.
- Adds docs: “Retention & Purge Enforcement Requirements” (DB/index/object storage, job runner, permissions, monitoring)

Validation
- Install on kind + minikube profiles: chart renders with and without optional stores.
- Values schema validation (helm lint + kubeconform) updated for new fields.
- Smoke test: retention config is visible in control plane and purge job CronJobs are created when enabled.

Rollout
- Recommended to land before enabling retention controls by default in Private distributions. Backward compatible (existing values files remain valid; new defaults apply when fields absent).

title: Private installer: add backup format versioning, restore compatibility checks, and migration/operator messaging
preview:
Context: We’ve had a few confusing restore failures in staging and in at least one on-prem customer scenario (SUP-18477) where the operator did not realize the backup artifact was produced by an older installer/runtime and required either (a) a restore-time migration step, or (b) a compatible installer version. This PR introduces explicit backup format versioning and a restore-time compatibility gate, plus clearer operator-facing errors and a short “migration notes” doc to reduce Support/SRE escalation time.

What this PR does (high level): 1) Adds a required `format_version` field to the backup manifest and embeds it into the artifact metadata, 2) Adds compatibility checks early in `restore apply` (before doing any destructive actions), including minimum/maximum supported versions and a readable “why” message, 3) Introduces explicit error codes and remediation hints (upgrade/downgrade installer, run a migration helper, verify CRDs/Helm charts), 4) Adds `--allow-unsupported-backup` escape hatch with loud warnings + telemetry marker, 5) Adds MIGRATIONS.md documenting known format deltas and how to handle them.

Backup format changes: - Backup manifest now contains: `format_version`, `created_by.installer_version`, `created_by.git_sha`, `created_at`, and `components[]` entries with per-component schema versions. - Default `format_version` is `2` for new backups. - Restore still supports `format_version=1` (from PR-482 era artifacts) with read-only “compatibility translation” for a small subset of fields.

Compatibility policy (as implemented): - Installer supports restoring backups with `format_version` in `[min_supported, max_supported]` defined in `internal/backup/compat/compat.go`. - If `format_version` is below `min_supported`, restore exits with `RWBI-RESTORE-0007` and guidance to use an older installer OR run an offline migration tool (if available). - If `format_version` is above `max_supported`, restore exits with `RWBI-RESTORE-0008` and guidance to upgrade the installer bundle to a newer release. - For on-prem/air-gapped, the message explicitly calls out “use the installer bundle shipped with the same release train as the backup” and points to `docs/MIGRATIONS.md` shipped in the bundle.

Operator-facing errors (examples): - RBAC/CRD ordering errors now include detected missing kinds and a suggested command to verify: `kubectl get crd | grep redwood` (best-effort). - S3/NFS backend errors include the resolved backend type and a pointer to the config key path that was missing/invalid. - KMS envelope encryption misconfig includes a hint to confirm KMS reachability and key policy (but does not print key IDs or sensitive material).

CLI changes: - `backup create` now writes `format_version` to the manifest and prints a one-line summary: `Backup created: format=v2 installer=0.18.3 artifact=...` - `restore apply` now performs compatibility checks immediately after reading and verifying the manifest checksum (before acquiring cluster locks). - New flags: `restore apply --allow-unsupported-backup` (defaults false), `restore apply --print-compat` (prints min/max supported and exits 0).

Security considerations: - No change to the encryption primitives in this PR; this only adds metadata + checks. - The escape hatch flag is intentionally noisy and logs an event marker for auditability. - Error messages avoid leaking secrets/paths beyond what’s required for remediation.

Testing: - Unit tests for compatibility matrix + manifest parsing. - Integration test adds a `fixtures/backup_v1/` artifact to ensure v1 restores are still accepted and surfaced as “legacy” in logs. - Manual: restored v1 and v2 backups in staging; verified we fail fast on an artificially bumped format_version.

Rollout notes: - New backups created after this merge are v2. - Restores remain backward compatible to v1; we should keep v1 support until we’re past the current customer upgrade window.

Related: - PR-482 (backup create), PR-489 (restore apply workflow + validation), PR-497 (air-gapped import/export).

CLI changes: - `backup create` now writes `format_version` to the manifest and prints a one-line summary: `Backup created: format=v2 installer=0.18.3 artifact=...` - `restore apply` now performs compatibility checks immediately after reading and verifying the manifest checksum (before acquiring cluster locks). - New flags: `restore apply --allow-unsupported-backup` (defaults false), `restore apply --print-compat` (prints min/max supported and exits 0).

Security considerations: - No change to the encryption primitives in this PR; this only adds metadata + checks. - The escape hatch flag is intentionally noisy and logs an event marker for auditability. - Error messages avoid leaking secrets/paths beyond what’s required for remediation.

Testing: - Unit tests for compatibility matrix + manifest parsing. - Integration test adds a `fixtures/backup_v1/` artifact to ensure v1 restores are still accepted and surfaced as “legacy” in logs. - Manual: restored v1 and v2 backups in staging; verified we fail fast on an artificially bumped format_version.

Rollout notes: - New backups created after this merge are v2. - Restores remain backward compatible to v1; we should keep v1 support until we’re past the current customer upgrade window.

Related: - PR-482 (backup create), PR-489 (restore apply workflow + validation), PR-497 (air-gapped import/export).

title: Introduce DNS-forwarder shadowing and deterministic SG naming for private control plane
preview: Motivation: Private control plane rollouts sometimes experience DNS transient failure during cross-account peering and SG rotation. This PR introduces a lightweight, opt-in "shadowed" DNS forwarder that mirrors control plane DNS zones into a customer-managed private hosted zone and a deterministic security-group naming scheme to make policy and rotation idempotent across re-applies. What changed: Terraform module additions (control-plane/dns-forwarder, network/sg-naming); Helm chart values to enable the forwarder as a DaemonSet for on-prem installs; an upgrade job that creates provisional shadow zones and validates DNS propagation; a small operator-side hook for safe SG swaps during migration. Rollout plan: Default behavior is unchanged. Operators can enable the feature via the private-installer helm values or by setting enable_shadow_dns = true in the Terraform module. Rollout steps are documented in upgrade_notes.md and include preflight checks, staged canary in a single AZ, and automated rollback if propagation or endpoint auth fails. Backwards compatibility: The DNS forwarder is additive; when disabled the installer behaves identically to previous releases. The SG naming scheme will not change existing SGs unless the migration job is explicitly run; migration is reversible. Security considerations: forwarder binds to VPC endpoints only (ENIs are ephemeral) and uses cross-account endpoint authorization tokens; IAM policy changes are scoped and documented. Related work: ties into ENG-7221 (dns resiliency) and ENG-7304 (safer sg rotation).

title: offline-bootstrap: OCI index + terraform mirror module for air-gapped registry bootstrapping
preview: Motivation: customers deploying Redwood Private in fully air-gapped datacenters requested a more deterministic bootstrap flow for registries and OS images. Current airgap bundles are large and monolithic which makes incremental updates and signature verification awkward during upgrades. This PR introduces a compact OCI image index format for runtime bundles, and a Terraform module to provision a local mirror + registry-signing step in offline environments. Changes include: 1) a new 'oci-index' manifest generator that emits per-arch component descriptors (runtime images, charts, kernel modules) and an LRU-aware delta manifest pointer for efficient staged upgrades; 2) a Terraform module redwood-terraform/registry-mirror which wires up a local registry, preloads index manifests, and produces a signed admission config used by the installer; 3) Helm chart hooks to verify image signatures using cosign during pre-install, and a lightweight pre-pull controller that validates manifests against the pinned index; 4) installer upgrade/rollback flow updates: atomic manifest pinning with symlinked index bundles and a fast rollback hook that swaps pointer files and triggers a controlled reconcile. This description includes implementation notes, test plan, and rollout strategy. Implementation notes: the oci-index generator is written in Go and produces index.json alongside OCI blobs; each entry includes a semantic component tag (eg runtime:v1.42.0) and a digest pin. The Terraform module supports grabbing index artifacts from an offline bundle, uploading to a local registry, and producing a signed keypair bundle (KMS compatible) for the installer to verify at runtime. The Helm hook runs as a pre-install Job that verifies signatures and pre-pulls images into node-local cache. Testing: unit tests added for index generator, integration tests for the Terraform module using localbin (kind + registry) in CI, and an optional e2e nightly airgapped pipeline that exercises upgrade/rollback. Rollout: feature gated behind installer flag --offline-oci-index and disabled by default; will be exposed to customers as experimental for two releases. Checklist: - [x] OCI index generator (Go) - [x] Terraform registry-mirror module - [x] Helm pre-install signature verification hook - [x] Installer manifest pin/rollback hooks - [x] Unit/integration tests - [x] Docs and upgrade notes Backwards compatibility: existing monolithic bundles are still supported by the installer; when --offline-oci-index is not enabled, bootstrapping behaves exactly as before. Security: cosign is used to verify images; private key material is expected to live in customer KMS and is never uploaded to Redwood systems. Related: Linear ENG-3942 (airgap registry UX), ENG-4037 (upgrade safety).

title: Add `restore apply` workflow with preflight checks and post-restore validation report
preview: Implements the end-to-end restore path for Redwood Private in the installer CLI. This adds a new `restore apply` command that: (1) performs strict preflight checks (cluster connectivity, namespace/CRD readiness, version compatibility, storage backend access, and integrity checks on the backup artifact), (2) restores control-plane state in a deterministic order (cluster prerequisites, control-plane chart values/config, DB restore, then service rollout), and (3) runs post-restore health checks and emits a validation report that Support/SRE can use for escalation. The goal is to make restores repeatable, safer to run under time pressure, and easier to debug when something goes wrong. Restore ordering is intentionally conservative to avoid the on-prem issues we saw in SUP-18477 (missing CRDs/incorrect bootstrap order). Security note: we do not restore secret material from backups; we restore secret references and validate that required KMS/external secret providers are reachable. The report includes sanitized environment details (versions, timestamps, artifact IDs), step-by-step results, and pointers to relevant logs. Usage examples: `private-installer restore apply --artifact s3://<bucket>/backups/<id> --namespace redwood-private --report /tmp/restore-report.json` and `private-installer restore apply --artifact ./backup.tgz.enc --offline --validate-only`.

title: Support air-gapped backup artifact import/export (offline mode + local checksum verification)
preview: ## Summary This PR adds first-class support for moving backup artifacts into/out of air-gapped environments with deterministic, locally verifiable integrity checks. It introduces: (1) `backup export` to package a previously created backup artifact + manifest into a portable bundle directory (or tar), (2) `backup import` to ingest a bundle into an air-gapped target cluster/operator workstation without any outbound calls, and (3) an explicit `--offline` mode that hard-fails if code paths attempt network access (including accidental cloud metadata calls). The goal is to enable on-prem/air-gapped customers to follow a repeatable workflow: create backup -> export to removable media -> import on restore site -> restore apply. Related: ENG-9829 (air-gapped bundle signing/verification follow-ups), ENG-9821 (`backup create`), customer issue context in SUP-18477 (restore failures due to missing/incorrectly staged artifacts). ## Motivation Air-gapped environments frequently have: no DNS resolution, blocked outbound egress, restricted IAM/no cloud credentials, and strict requirements for offline verification (checksums/signatures) prior to use. Prior `backup create` output was usable, but the workflow required manual copying and ad-hoc verification, and parts of the installer could still attempt network access depending on storage backend configuration. ## Design / Behavior - Bundle format: a directory containing `manifest.json` plus the encrypted payload(s) referenced by the manifest. The manifest includes: toolkit version, backup format version, created timestamp, file list, and SHA256 checksums per file. - `backup export --backup-id <id> --out <dir|tar>`: copies artifacts from the local cache/artifact store into the output bundle, writes/updates manifest checksums, and prints a copy-pasteable verification command. - `backup import --from <dir|tar> --into <cache>`: validates manifest schema, verifies checksums, enforces path traversal guards (no `..`, no absolute paths), and then stages files into the installer cache for subsequent `restore apply`. - `--offline` mode: disables any remote storage resolvers and fails fast if a backend requiring network is configured. Also disables AWS IMDS probing and any default credential chain that triggers metadata calls. - Local verification: `backup verify --bundle <dir>` (new subcommand) verifies checksums and prints a summary suitable for attaching to a ticket/escalation. ## Operator workflow example 1) On source site: `redwood-private-installer backup create --output s3://...` (or local) 2) Export: `redwood-private-installer backup export --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --out ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/` 3) Transfer directory via approved offline mechanism 4) On restore site (air-gapped): `redwood-private-installer backup import --from ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/ --offline` 5) Restore: `redwood-private-installer restore apply --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --offline` ## Implementation notes - Introduced `pkg/offline/bundle` helpers: manifest read/write, sha256 streaming, copy with fsync, atomic temp file writes. - Added `--offline` flag plumbing into the storage resolver layer; in offline mode we only permit `file://` URIs and local bundle sources. - Added explicit guardrails to prevent accidentally reading from `s3://` or `gs://` during import/verify when `--offline` is set. - Added unit tests for checksum verification, manifest schema validation, and path traversal rejection. - Added lightweight docs under `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` describing bundle layout, verification, and troubleshooting. ## Files / areas changed - `cmd/backup_export.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_import.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_verify.go` (new) - `pkg/offline/bundle/*` (new) - `pkg/storage/resolver.go` (offline gating) - `pkg/security/checksums.go` (sha256 helpers) - `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` (guidance + FAQ) - `docs/cli.md` (command reference updates) ## Testing - Unit: `go test ./...` includes new bundle tests - Manual: exported/imported bundle on a laptop with `REDWOOD_INSTALLER_OFFLINE=1` and verified no outbound calls (validated with `tcpdump` showing no SYNs, and by forcing DNS failure). - Negative tests: corrupted payload -> checksum mismatch; manifest missing file -> fails; malicious path `../../etc/passwd` -> rejected. ## Follow-ups (not included) - Bundle signing + signature verification (GPG/cosign) will be done in ENG-9829; this PR only covers checksums to close the immediate workflow gap. - Integrate `backup import` into the on-prem offline installer bundle (Release Engineering). ## Checklist - [x] No external calls in offline flow - [x] Checksum verification + clear operator output - [x] Docs for air-gapped workflow - [x] Tests for traversal + corruption cases

title: Add envelope encryption KMS provider interface (AWS reference impl + secure defaults)
preview:
Context: Private Day-2 backup/restore requires encryption at rest with customer-managed keys where applicable. The installer currently produces backup artifacts (tarball + manifest), but encryption is not pluggable and we can’t support customer KMS/HSM constraints cleanly across VPC + on-prem. This PR introduces a small, testable abstraction for KMS-based envelope encryption and ships a single reference provider implementation for AWS KMS, with secure defaults and explicit failure modes.

What’s in this PR: (1) New `kms.Provider` interface (GenerateDataKey/DecryptDataKey) that supports KMS-backed wrapping/unwrapping of a per-backup DEK (data encryption key). (2) New `crypto/envelope` package that performs streaming AES-256-GCM encryption of backup payloads using a DEK, storing only the wrapped key + nonce + algorithm metadata in the backup manifest. (3) AWS reference provider using `kms.GenerateDataKey`/`kms.Decrypt` with enforced `EncryptionContext` (backup_id, cluster_id, artifact_kind, format_version). (4) Secure defaults: AES-256-GCM, random 96-bit nonce, fail-closed if KMS is configured but unavailable, and redaction of any key material in logs/errors. (5) Tests: deterministic fake provider, manifest round-trip tests, and a “wrong context” decrypt failure test to prevent accidental weakening.

Non-goals (explicit): This PR does not wire envelope encryption into `backup create` yet (that’s handled in follow-up in ENG-9821/ENG-9823 integration work). This also does not attempt to support every KMS flavor immediately; the interface is designed so we can add GCP KMS / Azure Key Vault / on-prem HSM providers incrementally without touching encryption logic.

Operator-facing behavior: When enabled, backup artifacts include `encryption` metadata in `manifest.json` and the payload is emitted as `payload.enc` instead of `payload.tar.gz`. Restore requires access to the same KMS key (or a key that can decrypt the wrapped DEK) and the same encryption context fields. AWS provider configuration is via flags/env: `--kms-provider=aws`, `--kms-key-arn=...`, optional `--kms-region=...`. If `--kms-provider` is set, the tool will refuse to proceed without a key ARN.

Security notes: We do not store plaintext DEKs anywhere on disk; DEKs live in-memory for the encryption session. Wrapped keys are stored in the manifest (base64). We rely on KMS for KEK lifecycle/rotation. We intentionally treat KMS failures as hard failures (no silent downgrade) to avoid producing unencrypted backups unexpectedly.

Follow-ups: (a) integrate into backup/restore commands + Helm CronJob values (ENG-9823/ENG-9824), (b) document IAM least-privilege policy for AWS KMS usage (ENG-9825), (c) add “provider health” preflight for scheduled backups so we fail early with actionable errors.

title: Add upgrade preflight gate + rollback hints for enterprise private upgrades
preview:
Context: Private upgrades were relying on tribal knowledge for preflight checks and rollback readiness. This PR adds a first-class `upgrade preflight` command and standardizes the minimum gates we expect before an enterprise window starts. The intent is to align the tool output with the Confluence go/no-go checklist and the Change Management policy.

What changed: (1) New command `rpi upgrade preflight` that runs cluster readiness, API deprecation checks, cert expiry checks, and audit-log sink reachability checks. (2) Adds `--evidence-file` flag to dump a text report for attachment to a Jira change record. (3) Adds inline rollback hints based on what was changed (control plane vs data plane).

Operator-facing behavior: The preflight exits non-zero on hard blockers. Warnings are printed for non-blocking findings and are intended to be explicitly acknowledged by the change owner.

Checklist: [x] Docs updated (README) [x] Unit tests [x] Manual test on staging EKS cluster [x] Security review requested

Links: Confluence: Private Deployments: Upgrade Go/No-Go Checklist (Enterprise); Confluence: Production Change Management Policy; Confluence: Secure SDLC: Security Sign-off for Customer Deployments.

title: private-installer: add `backup create` command (manifest + encrypted artifact + verification output)
preview: This PR introduces an initial `backup create` workflow in the Redwood Private installer CLI to support Day-2 operations and DR readiness. The command produces (1) a human-readable manifest (JSON) describing what was captured and how it was encrypted, and (2) an encrypted artifact (tarball) containing the control-plane state snapshot files and selected deterministic configuration inputs. The goal of this PR is to ship the core mechanics (consistent snapshot + packaging + encryption + basic verification/logging) so Solutions/SRE teams can begin rehearsing restores in staging while we iterate on storage backends, scheduled jobs, and restore automation. Scope: control-plane backup only (no data-plane model cache / no customer prompt data). Secrets are not exported as raw secret material; we only record references (e.g., external KMS key ARNs, secret names) and require the target cluster to have access to the same secret sources at restore time. The artifact format is intentionally versioned (`format_version: 1`) and includes integrity checks (SHA256) for each included file and the encrypted blob. Encryption is implemented as AES-256-GCM with a randomly generated data key; for this PR, the data key is derived from a user-provided passphrase via scrypt and stored only in-memory during packaging (no plaintext key material written to disk). A follow-up will add a KMS provider interface for envelope encryption (see ENG-9823) and backup storage backends (S3/NFS) integration. Documentation: adds CLI help text and a short internal README section describing the artifact layout and the restore prerequisites. text: This PR introduces an initial `backup create` workflow in the Redwood Private installer CLI to support Day-2 operations and DR readiness. The command produces (1) a human-readable manifest (JSON) describing what was captured and how it was encrypted, and (2) an encrypted artifact (tarball) containing the control-plane state snapshot files and selected deterministic configuration inputs. The goal of this PR is to ship the core mechanics (consistent snapshot + packaging + encryption + basic verification/logging) so Solutions/SRE teams can begin rehearsing restores in staging while we iterate on storage backends, scheduled jobs, and restore automation. Scope: control-plane backup only (no data-plane model cache / no customer prompt data). Secrets are not exported as raw secret material; we only record references (e.g., external KMS key ARNs, secret names) and require the target cluster to have access to the same secret sources at restore time. title: add-oci-prefetcher-and-kubelet-ephemeral-overlay-for-airgapped-bootstrap
preview:
Problem: air-gapped installs often spend excessive time pulling large runtime images and kernel modules during initial bootstrap. Existing delta snapshots help, but cluster bring-up still faces long cold starts and fragile manual registry seeding.

Solution: introduce an OCI prefetcher + index format plus a thin ephemeral kubelet overlay that allows the installer to stream and stage only required image layers and kubelet artifacts into a temporary local store before handing control to the normal runtime. Key components in this PR: (1) installer/terraform module to create a prefetcher DaemonSet and registry proxy for sealed bundles; (2) a compact OCI index format and tooling (installer/tools/oci-indexer) that produces layered prefetch manifests optimized for minimal disk churn; (3) helm chart additions that render a kubelet-ephemeral overlay (charts/redwood-private/templates/kubelet-ephemeral.yaml) and init hooks to populate an overlayfs during airgap bootstrap; (4) preflight + smoke tests and docs for operators.

What changed (high level):
- Add installer/tools/oci-indexer and a small Go binary that walks OCI archives and emits prefetch manifests containing ordered layer digests and platform hints.
- Add installer/scripts/prefetch-service.sh and a lightweight systemd unit template used by the bootstrapper to start an ephemeral prefetcher when offline caches are empty.
- Terraform: installer/terraform/modules/prefetcher - module to deploy the prefetcher, registry proxy, and RBAC artifacts for private installs.
- Helm: charts/redwood-private chart + values to opt into ephemeral kubelet overlay and prefetcher sidecar (default OFF).
- Installer: hook to consume index manifests and drive parallel layer download with an adaptive concurrency limiter to avoid OOM on small nodes.
- Docs: docs/airgap/prefetcher.md with operator instructions and upgrade notes.

Checklist: 
- [x] Add prefetcher binary + tests
- [x] Terraform module with example usage
- [x] Helm templates and values gating
- [x] Integration smoke test for airgap bootstrap
- [x] Docs and upgrade notes

Compatibility and rollout:
- Feature gated (values.prefetcher.enabled=false by default) so existing clusters are unaffected.
- Installer will use the prefetcher automatically when values.prefetcher.enabled=true and a sealed-bundle index is provided.

Notes on safety: prefetcher runs as non-privileged user with limited caps; the ephemeral kubelet overlay uses overlayfs mounted to a temporary path and automatically unmounted after promotion. We included an explicit rollback hook that removes staged layers if the bootstrap fails, avoiding disk pollution.

Merge: Squash-merged to main after two approvals and green CI.

Related: Linear ENG-8844 tracks follow-ups for multi-arch prefetch manifests and stronger integrity checking. SUP-2201 is the support ticket that motivated the work (customer reported 2+ hour cold bootstrap on baremetal with strict egress).

title: manifest rollup + attestation gating: canonical index and CI enforcement
preview:
Motivation: Private-installer releases need a single canonical manifest index that the CI can attest and gate on before publishing to customers. This PR implements a manifest rollup transformer that ingests per-arch/per-release artifact metadata, computes a deterministic canonical index (content-addressed ordering + delta graph), emits an attestable bundle, and wires an optional CI gating check that rejects a release if attestations are missing or provenance fails verification.

This is intended to reduce the number of manual steps in private-installer release runs, make rollbacks deterministic (by publishing a signed index snapshot), and give SREs/Customers cryptographic proof of the exact artifacts applied by an upgrade. Related Linear: ENG-4379 (manifest rollup) and ENG-4391 (attestation policy).

What this PR does (high level):
- Adds manifest-rollup binary and library used in the release pipeline to compose a single canonical manifest index from per-artifact metadata.
- Produces a deterministic index file (index.json.sha256) plus an attestable bundle (index.tar.gz.sig + provenance.json) suitable for cosign/canonical verification.
- Integrates a new CI gating job (release-index-verify) in .github/workflows/release.yml that verifies signatures, provenance chains, and KMS references before allowing the release job to proceed.
- Adds a backfill migration script to regenerate canonical indexes for a small subset of historical releases (used in the migration smoke tests).
- Documents the attestation policy and failure modes in docs/installer/release-attest.md.

Checklist: [x] unit + integration tests for rollup logic, [x] end-to-end CI check wired, [x] docs updated, [x] security review acknowledged.

Notes: The design favors deterministic ordering over human-readable diffs (index is content-addressed). The attestation policy is configurable per-env (see installer/release-attest.md). If the KMS path is unavailable the gate fails closed; we ship a temporary bypass keyed to a Linear ticket for emergency rollouts but it is disabled by default.

title: transactional-release-apply: add TUF-backed transactional locks and manifest staging
preview:
Problem: release application for private-installer can partially apply manifests when concurrent pipelines overlap (e.g. two builds push near-simultaneously). This has caused transient mismatches between the published release manifest and the in-cluster helm state, forcing manual rollbacks. Goal: make release manifest publishing and cluster apply transactional and idempotent so that either the entire manifest set is applied (and published) or nothing changes, with a short, observable staging window and an automatic rollback marker if something fails.

What this PR does (single string): Adds a TUF-backed transactional lock and a manifest staging area to the private-installer release pipeline. The new flow stages a release bundle under a provisional manifest name (manifest:vN-staging), obtains a transactional advisory lock via the TUF metadata store (using conditional metadata updates and an ephemeral lock role), performs preflight validation (diff checks, schema/helm lint, and cluster dry-run against the target kubeconfig), attempts a staged helm apply using --wait and a short timeout, and upon success atomically promotes the staged manifest to the canonical manifest name (manifest:vN) using a signed metadata rotation step. If promotion fails or preflight fails, we write a rollback marker file (manifest:vN-rollback) and garbage collect the staged artifacts after a TTL. The implementation is intentionally conservative: timeouts and retries are tunable via pipeline variables and default to conservative values to reduce false positives.

Changes included: 1) CI pipeline: new release-transactional job (gitlab/worker) that stages artifacts and orchestrates TUF metadata updates; 2) TUF tooling: add tuf-lock CLI and small library helpers for conditional role updates and ephemeral lock roles; 3) Installer: manifest staging path support and rollback marker handling in the private-installer repo; 4) Helm: chart hooks updated to treat staged annotations specially and to emit structured apply result records; 5) Terraform: adds a data source that can query the canonical manifest state for automation hooks; 6) Tests: end-to-end CI integration test simulating concurrent publishes, and unit tests around lock edge-cases.

Why this approach: We considered a pure datastore advisory lock but chose to build on top of our existing TUF metadata store to ensure provenance and signed atomicity of promotion steps. This gives auditability (signed metadata changes) and prevents race conditions across regions that rely on the same artifact index. The staged manifest approach also preserves a traceable artifact lineage for rollbacks and postmortem.

Checklist: - [x] Add staged manifest path and promotion API - [x] Implement tuf-lock CLI + library - [x] Hook promotion to signed metadata rotation - [x] Add rollback marker and GC policy - [x] Integration tests for concurrent publish - [x] Update release docs and runbook snippets - [x] CI job and RBAC for pipeline to sign/push metadata

Notes and migration: Existing release consumers that read manifest:vN remain unchanged. New consumers can query manifest:vN-staging when they want to validate in a staging environment. The pipeline introduces two new environment variables (RELEASE_LOCK_TTL and STAGING_TTL) with reasonable defaults. We also rotate one ephemeral TUF key used for lock leases; the rotation step is included and gated behind a feature flag for the first rollout.

title: implement-tf-module-gpu-nodepool-lease-index-and-iam-role-template-rotation
preview:
Context and motivation: Private deployments need consistent, deterministic allocation of GPU nodepools across tenants while preserving immutability in IAM role configuration for auditability. Current tenant lease allocation can drift when nodes are recreated or TF state is sharded, causing tenant-to-node affinity regressions and noisy autoscaler behavior. This PR introduces a new Terraform module that provides a deterministic `lease_index` allocator for GPU nodepools and an IAM role templating system that creates immutable role versions with automated rotation hooks.

What this PR does: 1) Adds module `tfmodules/gpu_lease_index` implementing a lease-index allocation strategy that: computes a stable index for tenant leases based on tenant-id hashing + ordered subnet slices; emits outputs consumed by the nodepool provisioning module to ensure stable assignment even during partial applies and state sharding. 2) Adds module `tfmodules/iam_role_template` which renders IAM role artifacts as immutable-versioned resources (role_v1, role_v2) and wires a controlled rotation mechanism via a small controller job (K8s Job manifest + preflight plan) that can be driven by `private-installer upgrade`. 3) Provides a migration tool `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate` (dry-run + apply modes) to compute and persist lease_index mappings into a new state namespace, with safety checks and a --rollback snapshot option. 4) Adds tagging for GPU nodepool instances to surface lease_index and role_version for drift detection and reconciler diagnostics. 5) Updates Helm hooks in `charts/private-installer` to add post-upgrade validation for IAM rotations and nodepool tag presence.

Design notes: - Lease index derivation uses a stable HMAC(nonce=deployment-id, key=infra-hash) truncated to the pool size to ensure stable ordering across applies. The TF module exposes both `lease_index` and `lease_salt` so downstream modules can detect mismatches and trigger safe rebinds. - IAM role templating intentionally creates new role resources instead of in-place edits; the rotation controller sequences traffic to roles by annotating service accounts, running preflight checks, then promoting a new version, and finally deleting the old role only when safe. - The migration tool does not alter live node labels; it only writes the lease_index mapping into the TF state file and produces a reconciliation plan for the installer to apply.

Commits: ["a1b9f7c add gpu_lease_index module and unit tests", "b4d3e82 add iam_role_template module and rotation job manifest", "f7c2a10 add tfstate-lease-index-migrate script with dry-run", "e3f9b5a update helm chart hooks for post-upgrade validation", "c9d0aa4 add nodepool tagging and reconciler metrics", "d2f8f4b docs: migration and rollout notes"]

Files changed (high level): ["tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/main.tf", "tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/outputs.tf", "tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/README.md", "tfmodules/iam_role_template/main.tf", "tfmodules/iam_role_template/rotation-job.yaml", "scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate", "charts/private-installer/templates/post-upgrade-validation.yaml", "charts/private-installer/values.schema.json", "modules/nodepool/attach_tags.tf", "modules/nodepool/README.md", "docs/private-deploy/migration-lease-index.md", "tests/lease_index/unit_tests.go"]

Testing and CI: - Unit tests for lease-index math (Go) and TF unit-style checks included; added integration test to create a two-tenant private deployment and assert stable assignment across repeated applies with shuffled state shards. - CI initially failed due to a lint rule on HCL variable naming; fixed and re-ran. Final CI run: all checks passed (plan acceptance, fmt, terratest, unit).

Review thread summary: - Arun: requested explicit docs on operator runbook for manual rotation fallback; added `docs/private-deploy/iam-rotation-runbook.md` and a small CLI helper. - Sophie: asked to add a preflight check that verifies service account annotation propagation; added contract test and Helm hook. - Mateo: flagged potential permission escalation during rotation; updated IAM policies to require `iam:PassRole` only scoped to role_version resource names and added audit logging. - Reviewer approvals: Arun approved after runbook added, Sophie approved after tests; Mateo requested one follow-up (see TODO).

Migration plan and rollout: 1) Run `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate --dry-run` in staging to preview mappings. 2) Apply mapping with `--apply` to write to a new state namespace (non-destructive) and run `private-installer upgrade --validate`. 3) Observe reconciler metrics: if any tenant reports lease mismatch, the reconciler will temporarily create a shadow nodepool and migrate workloads before deleting the old nodepool (safe by default). 4) IAM rotation: default is manual and gated; operator can run `private-installer iam rotate --role <template> --promote` which triggers the rotation job. A fully-automated rollout is behind a feature flag.

Rollback: - The migration tool produces a snapshot file that can be used to revert state; the `--rollback` flag re-applies the snapshot into TF backend. - IAM rotation includes a rollback path: the controller keeps the previous role until operator confirms traffic has drained.

Risk and mitigation: - Risk: mismatch between lease_index and live node labels during partial apply. Mitigation: tagging + reconciler detection; migration initially run in dry-run and then staged apply; daily reconciliation window during first week. - Risk: accidental role deletion. Mitigation: immutable role versions retained for 7 days by default and deletion requires explicit operator approval recorded in the runbook.

Follow-ups and TODO: - ENG-7460: automated promotion pipeline for IAM rotation (epic). - Add metrics for lease_index churn to SLO dashboards. - Lightweight UI in Console for lease mapping visualization (product ticket).

Merge outcome: merged via squash after approvals and CI green.

title: introduce-interactive-migration-safepoints-and-rollback-fence
preview:
Motivation: Private deployments need a safer, operator-driven way to perform schema migrations and rollbacks across multi-tenant clusters. Previous upgrades occasionally left clusters in a state where a naive downgrade could break tenant DB schemas or leave a partial logical replication setup. This PR introduces (1) an interactive safepoint API exposed by the operator to mark tenant migration safepoints, (2) a rollback-fence that prevents downgrades when incompatible schema delta exists, and (3) a set of Helm and Terraform guard checks to ensure plan/upgrade parity before applying changes.

Summary of changes (single-line): interactive safepoint operator API, rollback-fence enforcement, helm preflight hook to quiesce workloads, timeline-aware postgres snapshotter and restore verifier, terraform state version gate.

Checklist (for merge):
- [x] Operator: add Safepoint CRD + controller to record migration checkpoints and upstream WAL position
- [x] Installer: implement rollback-fence logic that reads last safepoint and schema-compat index
- [x] Helm chart: add pre-upgrade hook that quiesces user-facing processes and snapshots logical replication slots
- [x] Terraform: add plan-time check that validates cluster manifest hash and fails apply if state version mismatches
- [x] E2E: add integration test that performs upgrade -> set safepoint -> simulate downgrade attempt -> assert fence blocks downgrade
- [x] Docs: write runbook for interactive safepoint operations and emergency rollback flow

Detailed description: The operator now exposes a new CRD called RedwoodMigrationSafepoint which operators (or the CLI) can create to mark a tenant-aware migration checkpoint. Each safepoint records a cluster-scoped manifest hash, postgres timeline ID, LSN, and an optional human message. The safepoint is written atomically and also persisted to the installer metadata store. During upgrades, the operator will record an automatic pre-migration safepoint (when enabled) and annotate it into the release metadata. The rollback-fence consults the latest safepoint and a lightweight schema-compatibility index (stored as a manifest label) before permitting any Helm/Terraform-driven downgrade. If the fence detects a schema delta that cannot be auto-downgraded, it rejects the operation with an actionable error and links to the runbook.

Helm changes: added hooks/ pre-upgrade-job.yaml which calls operator apis: operatorctl quiesce --release <release> --timeout 5m and operatorctl snapshot --tenant <tenant> to produce a consistent logical snapshot. Hook is non-blocking by default but can be forced via values: preflight.quiesce=true. Also added post-rollback-validation hook that waits for WAL replay and checksum verification.

Terraform changes: new module variable manifest_lock_hash and pre-apply validation that calls the installer API for expected state. A sentinel file is written to the cluster object store as a short-lived plan-lease; terraform apply will fail if another active plan-lease exists or if the manifest hash differs. This prevents divergent state.apply sequences across CI and manual runs.

Postgres snapshotter & restore: add timeline-aware logical snapshotter which captures timeline ID and LSN alongside snapshot tarball. Restore verifier replays WAL up to the LSN and then runs a fast integrity check (row counts + schema checksums) for configured critical tables. The restore path includes a --dry-verify mode for customers to run a low-risk restore verification before committing.

Runbook & UX: documented operator CLI verbs: safepoint create/list/delete, operatorctl fence-status, operatorctl force-unlock (requires 2 approvals). The runbook includes step-by-step rollback example: create safepoint -> take snapshot -> attempt downgrade (blocked) -> follow remediation steps (roll-forward migration, rollback gate rescue).

Commits (high-level):
- Add RedwoodMigrationSafepoint CRD and controller scaffolding
- Implement safepoint persistence and manifest-hash annotation
- Add rollback-fence service and API endpoints
- Helm: pre-upgrade quiesce hook + post-rollback verifier
- Terraform: plan-lease & manifest-hash preflight check
- Postgres: timeline-aware snapshotter and WAL replay verifier
- Tests: e2e upgrade/downgrade integration and unit tests for fence logic

Changed areas (representative files):
- operator/api/v1alpha1/migration_safepoint.go
- operator/controllers/safepoint_controller.go
- installer/services/rollback_fence.go
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/pre-upgrade-quiesce-job.yaml
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/post-rollback-verify-job.yaml
- terraform/modules/cluster/main.tf (plan-lease check helper)

title: airgap: kubelet provisioning + OCI registry mirroring + reproducible delta bundles
preview: Motivation: customers running fully air-gapped Private deployments reported two recurring pain points: (1) manual kubelet/node provisioning steps that vary by distro, and (2) brittle offline registry mirroring that broke during incremental upgrades. This PR delivers: 1) a cross-distro kubelet provisioning helper that generates systemd unit snippets + containerd mirror configs from the airgap catalog and injects them into the node bootstrap bundle; 2) an OCI registry mirror manifest generator that produces deterministic image manifest lists and mirror index files so mirror replication can be validated offline; 3) a reproducible delta-bundle generator that emits compact binary diffs between runtime release bundles to reduce s3/usb transfer sizes and speed upgrades in low-bandwidth datacenters; 4) an upgrade/rollback hook that stages kubelet config changes and can atomically swap systemd unit drop-ins to avoid mid-upgrade kubelet restarts. Changes are guarded behind the new installer flag --airgap-kubelet-mirror. Backwards compatibility: installers that don't enable the flag see no change in bundle layout. Safety: all generated artifacts are signed with the installer GPG key and the installer now emits a verification manifest with sha256 sums of each artifact. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for manifest generator - [x] integration smoke test with k3s and rke2 bundles - [x] installer e2e in airgapped CI matrix - [x] upgrade/rollback scenario tested on VM lab. Known limitations: kubelet helper currently supports systemd-based distros (Ubuntu/Fedora/RHEL/CentOS/Alma) and containerd; non-systemd distros will fallback to a documented manual step. See ENG-4783 for next steps (non-systemd support, Windows nodes). Deployment notes: operators must add the generated mirror index to their private registry seed; rollback is automatic only if the generated delta patch applies cleanly. Links: runway doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4783/airgap-kubelet-mirroring, internal S3 bucket path s3://airgap-builds/delta/2024-10-12/.

title: atomic-kms-key-promotion-with-grace-period-rollback-hooks
preview: Motivation: On private deployments customers run HSM-backed KMS with periodic key rotations. Past rotations that involved immediate primary key swaps caused brief failures during leader election and sealed-secrets unseal handoffs in air-gapped clusters. This PR implements an "atomic key promotion" flow plus a configurable grace period and automated rollback hooks to ensure safe cutover without downtime.
